text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

## Context management (automatic)

To keep your context small and your runs cheap, the harness automatically
elides the *raw text* of large `read`/`bash` outputs after they have been on
screen for a few turns, replacing each with a short `[context-saver: …]` stub
that names the file or command. The document was fully read when it was
current — nothing was hidden from your initial pass — only the bulk is dropped
from later turns. Two habits keep this lossless:

- As you read a document, **record the specific facts, figures, dates, and
  verbatim clauses you will need** into your notes or draft. Don't rely on the
  full text staying resident.
- If you later need the exact text of a doc that now shows a `[context-saver:
  …]` stub, simply **`read` that file again** — the full content returns.

## File-format skills

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.


## Skill: docx

---
name: docx
description: "Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word)."
---

# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |
| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |
| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |
| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |
| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |
| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |
| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |
| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |

All scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.

## Creating a new document

Pick by what you have:

- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).
- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.
- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.

When unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.

## Editing an existing document

Three-step pattern:

```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/word/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Key files inside the unpacked tree:

- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)
- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions
- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)
- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)
- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers
- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.
- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.

### Run-merging gotcha

Word writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.

### Smart-quote escaping

Microsoft Word uses smart quotes (`"` `"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.

### Whitespace preservation

Trailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space="preserve"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.

## Redlines (tracked changes)

```bash
python scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \
  --author "Reviewer" --date "2026-04-30"
```

Default mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.

### Manual mode

If `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:
- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).
- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.
- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.

### What gets tracked

- Insertions and deletions of text
- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)
- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)

### What doesn't get tracked

- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)
- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)
- Image swaps

## Comments

```bash
python scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json
```

`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:
- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.
- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).
- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.

Anchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.

`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.

## Accept / reject changes

```bash
python scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx
```

Uses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:

```basic
Sub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub
```

LibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.

To reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**

```bash
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Checks:
- Round-trip ZIP integrity
- XML well-formedness for every part
- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs
- Content-type registration for every referenced part
- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)

Exit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.

## Common pitfalls

- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.
- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.
- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.
- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.
- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.
- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type="dxa">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.
- Signing / encryption / DRM.
- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).



## Skill: pptx

---
name: pptx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy)."
---

# PPTX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |
| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |
| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |
| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |
| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |
| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Generation modalities

**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):
```bash
node scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx
```
`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.

**Markdown via Marp**:
```bash
bash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx
```
Best for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.

**Programmatic via python-pptx**:
Best when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.

## Editing existing decks

Three-step pattern, like docx:
```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx
python scripts/validate.py output.pptx
```

For surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:
```bash
python scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \
  --slide 2 --shape "Title 1" --op set_text --value "New title"
```

JSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.

## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx

- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.
- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.
- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.
- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.
- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.

## Deterministic QA loop

After every generation, run:

```bash
python scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide
python scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json
```

`deterministic_qa.py` checks:
- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges
- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection
- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles
- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)
- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide

Output is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.

(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).
- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.
- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).
- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).



## Skill: xlsx

---
name: xlsx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel)."
---

# XLSX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |
| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |
| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |
| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |
| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)

Apply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:

- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.
- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.
- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.
- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* "-"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).
- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an "x" character. Format: `0.0"x"`.
- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.
- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.
- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `"$1,234M"` as a string.

`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.

## Formula authoring

- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.
- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names["assumptions"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.
- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.
- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.

## Recalculation — choose your engine

`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).

**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Drives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.

**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Uses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.

**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.

If you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.

## Error scan

After every recalc, scan for formula errors:

```bash
python scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json
```

Reports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.

If errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.

## Validation gate

`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).
- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.
- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.
- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.
- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.

**A. Whitmore\'s Acquisition of Cascade (2019)**

On January 8, 2019, Whitmore Fund III LP --- a private equity fund vehicle managed by Whitmore Capital Partners LLC --- acquired a 62% controlling interest in Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. for a purchase price of \$118 million. Whitmore Capital Partners LLC, acting as general partner and investment manager of Whitmore Fund III LP, directed the acquisition and negotiated its terms. Garrett T. Whitmore, the founder and Managing Partner of Whitmore Capital Partners, personally oversaw the acquisition strategy and approved the investment on behalf of Fund III.

At the time of the acquisition, Cascade was a profitable and growing manufacturer of industrial heat-exchange systems. The Company held fourteen (14) United States patents covering its proprietary ThermalCore technology, which represented the core of Cascade\'s intellectual property portfolio and the principal driver of its commercial value. Cascade had established a strong market position in the industrial heat-exchange sector, with revenues supported by both product sales and licensing of its ThermalCore technology to third-party customers at arm\'s-length rates.

The acquisition was structured through the Amended and Restated Operating Agreement, which replaced Cascade\'s prior governing documents. The Operating Agreement granted Whitmore Fund III LP --- and by extension, Whitmore Capital Partners LLC --- effective control over Cascade\'s Board of Directors, including the power to appoint a majority of the Board\'s members. The minority shareholders, holding a combined 38% of outstanding shares, retained their equity stakes subject to the Operating Agreement\'s governance provisions, including minority shareholder protections set forth in Section 8.7 (governing affiliate transactions) and the mandatory arbitration clause in Section 12.3.

Janet Okafor, the lead claimant representative in this proceeding, held 11.4% of Cascade\'s outstanding shares at the time of the acquisition. As part of the Operating Agreement\'s minority protection framework, Ms. Okafor was appointed to the Cascade Board of Directors as a representative of the minority shareholders, a position she has held continuously since January 2019.

**B. Cascade\'s ThermalCore Technology Portfolio**

Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. holds fourteen (14) United States patents related to advanced thermal management technology, collectively known as the \"ThermalCore\" technology portfolio. The ThermalCore technology encompasses proprietary heat-exchange systems designed for high-load industrial applications, including phase-change heat exchangers, advanced thermal transfer fluids, and integrated thermal control platforms. These technologies are used in demanding industrial environments, including chemical processing facilities, power generation plants, and large-scale manufacturing operations where precise thermal regulation is critical to operational efficiency and safety.

The ThermalCore technology was developed under the leadership of Dr. Rita Peshkov, Vice President of Research & Development at Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. Dr. Peshkov has served as VP of R&D at Cascade since prior to the Whitmore acquisition and is the named inventor or co-inventor on the majority of Cascade\'s fourteen patents. She is the Company\'s foremost technical authority on ThermalCore technology and its commercial applications. Dr. Peshkov holds a Ph.D. in mechanical engineering and has published extensively in the field of advanced thermal engineering, including in peer-reviewed journals such as the *Journal of Advanced Thermal Engineering*. Her expertise encompasses the design, testing, and commercialization of heat-exchange systems for industrial use, and she has been instrumental in translating Cascade\'s fundamental research into patented, commercially viable technology products.

Dr. Peshkov is anticipated to serve as a key expert witness for Claimants in this arbitration, providing testimony regarding: (a) the technical specifications, functionality, and uniqueness of ThermalCore technology; (b) the competitive advantages the technology confers on any licensee, including increased operational efficiency, reduced energy consumption, and enhanced product quality; (c) the absence of commercially viable substitutes for ThermalCore technology in the industrial heat-exchange market; and (d) the technical basis for the methodology used to value the technology license at fair market rates.

The ThermalCore technology is Cascade\'s most valuable asset and the cornerstone of its enterprise value. Prior to the execution of the disputed License Agreement with Volaris, Cascade had licensed ThermalCore technology to unrelated third-party licensees in arm\'s-length transactions at rates consistent with or exceeding the \$9.5 million per year valuation subsequently confirmed by Ridgeline Advisory Group. The technology\'s value derives from its proprietary nature, the breadth of patent protection, the absence of commercially viable alternatives, and the significant operational advantages it provides to licensees.

**C. The Volaris License Agreement (March 1, 2023)**

On March 1, 2023, Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. entered into a Technology License Agreement (the \"License Agreement\") granting Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC an exclusive license to use the ThermalCore technology for certain specified industrial applications. The License Agreement was not the product of arm\'s-length negotiation between unrelated parties. Rather, Whitmore Capital Partners LLC --- exercising its control over Cascade\'s Board of Directors through the 62% controlling interest held by Whitmore Fund III LP --- directed Cascade to enter into the License Agreement with Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC, a portfolio company of Whitmore Fund II LP, another fund managed by the same entity: Whitmore Capital Partners LLC.

The License Agreement set the annual license fee at \$2.8 million per year. The license term is seven (7) years, running from March 1, 2023 through February 28, 2030, resulting in total license payments over the full term of \$19.6 million (\$2.8 million multiplied by seven years). The license is exclusive for the specified applications, meaning that Cascade is prohibited from licensing ThermalCore technology to any other party for those same uses during the license term --- a restriction that compounds the harm to Cascade by foreclosing revenue from competitive licensees willing to pay market rates.

The License Agreement was approved by the Whitmore-controlled board majority over the express, written objection of minority shareholder representative Janet Okafor. On February 15, 2023 --- approximately two weeks before the License Agreement was executed --- Ms. Okafor submitted a written objection to the Cascade Board of Directors specifically flagging the self-dealing nature of the proposed transaction. In her objection, Ms. Okafor identified the conflict of interest inherent in Whitmore Capital Partners\' position as manager of both the fund controlling the licensor (Whitmore Fund III LP / Cascade) and the fund owning the licensee (Whitmore Fund II LP / Volaris). Ms. Okafor demanded that the Board obtain an independent valuation of the ThermalCore technology license before any agreement was executed with Volaris and that the transaction be approved only by disinterested directors, as required by Section 8.7 of the Operating Agreement.

The Whitmore-controlled board majority disregarded Ms. Okafor\'s objection and proceeded to approve and execute the License Agreement without obtaining an independent valuation at the time of execution. No fairness opinion was solicited. No disinterested director committee was convened. The approval was effected solely by the votes of board members appointed by Whitmore Fund III LP, over the sole dissenting vote of Ms. Okafor.

Garrett T. Whitmore personally directed the terms of the license through his management role at Whitmore Capital Partners, which exercises control over both Whitmore Fund II LP (which owns Volaris) and Whitmore Fund III LP (which owns 62% of Cascade). Thomas Kinnear, CEO of Volaris, negotiated on Volaris\'s behalf but acted under the direction and oversight of Whitmore Capital Partners as the fund manager controlling Volaris through its ownership by Whitmore Fund II LP. In substance, both sides of the transaction were controlled by the same entity --- Whitmore Capital Partners LLC --- and by the same individual --- Garrett T. Whitmore.

**D. The Ridgeline Advisory Group Valuation (November 2023)**

Following the execution of the License Agreement, and in light of the Board\'s refusal to commission an independent valuation prior to approval, the minority shareholders of Cascade retained Ridgeline Advisory Group (\"Ridgeline\"), an independent technology and intellectual property valuation firm, to assess the fair market value of the ThermalCore technology license. Ridgeline was retained in September 2023 and completed its valuation report on November 15, 2023.

Ridgeline determined that the fair market value of the ThermalCore technology license is \$9.5 million per year. This conclusion was based on a comprehensive analysis employing multiple valuation methodologies, including: (a) an analysis of comparable license transactions in the industrial heat-exchange sector, which demonstrated that licenses of comparable scope, exclusivity, and technological significance routinely command annual fees in the range identified; (b) a discounted cash flow analysis of the incremental revenue and cost savings that ThermalCore technology generates for a licensee, demonstrating significant economic benefit to any entity deploying the technology in industrial applications; (c) an assessment of the absence of commercially viable substitutes for ThermalCore technology, which supports premium pricing due to limited alternatives available to potential licensees; and (d) an evaluation of the exclusive nature of the license granted to Volaris, which forecloses Cascade from licensing the same technology to competing parties for the specified applications during the seven-year term.

The \$9.5 million per year fair market value represents the rate that would be agreed upon between a willing licensor and a willing licensee, each acting in their own interest and with full knowledge of the relevant facts, in an arm\'s-length transaction free of duress or undue influence. This figure stands in stark contrast to the \$2.8 million per year license fee imposed by Whitmore through its control over Cascade\'s Board.

The annual differential between the license rate under the License Agreement and the independently determined fair market value is \$6.7 million (\$9.5 million minus \$2.8 million). Over the full seven-year license term, the total value transferred from Cascade to Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC --- and through Volaris to Whitmore Fund II LP and Whitmore Capital Partners LLC --- amounts to \$46.9 million (\$6.7 million multiplied by seven years). This represents a massive wealth transfer between fund vehicles under common management, engineered at the direct expense of Cascade\'s minority shareholders. The value flows from an entity 62% owned by Whitmore Fund III LP (Cascade) to an entity owned by Whitmore Fund II LP (Volaris), with both funds managed by Whitmore Capital Partners LLC. The net result is that Whitmore Capital Partners enhances the portfolio value, performance metrics, and fee-generating capacity of one fund (Fund II) at the direct expense of investors in another fund (Fund III) and the minority shareholders of a Fund III portfolio company.

**E. Pre-Arbitration Demand and Failure to Cure**

On December 20, 2023, counsel for the minority shareholders --- Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP --- sent a formal written demand letter to Whitmore Capital Partners LLC (at its principal offices in Boston, Massachusetts) and to the Board of Directors of Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. The demand letter set forth the minority shareholders\' position that the License Agreement constituted a self-dealing transaction in violation of the Operating Agreement and Delaware fiduciary duty law, and it demanded three specific remedial actions: (a) rescission of the License Agreement in its entirety; (b) renegotiation of any future license of ThermalCore technology to Volaris at fair market rates, with independent oversight and disinterested director approval; and (c) disgorgement by Volaris of the license fee differential already received --- that is, the difference between the \$2.8 million per year license fee and the \$9.5 million per year fair market value for the period the License Agreement had been in effect.

On January 15, 2024, Whitmore Capital Partners LLC, through its counsel at Barrow & Thistlewood LLP, responded to the demand letter. The response denied any breach of fiduciary duty and asserted that the license fee of \$2.8 million per year was at commercially reasonable rates reflecting the scope and nature of the license granted. The response did not provide any independent valuation or other evidence to support this assertion. No remedial action of any kind was taken. The License Agreement remained in full force and effect at the challenged terms, and Volaris continued to exploit ThermalCore technology at the below-market rate.

Having exhausted their pre-arbitration demand obligations and having received no satisfaction, the minority shareholders filed this Arbitration Demand on March 15, 2024, invoking the mandatory arbitration provision of Section 12.3 of the Operating Agreement.

**[VI. CLAIMS FOR RELIEF]{.underline}**

**A. Count I --- Breach of Fiduciary Duty (Duty of Loyalty)**

1\. Claimants incorporate by reference all preceding paragraphs of this Demand as though set forth fully herein.

2\. As the controlling shareholder of Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. --- holding 62% of the Company\'s outstanding shares through Whitmore Fund III LP --- Whitmore Capital Partners LLC owes fiduciary duties of loyalty and care to Cascade and to its minority shareholders under Delaware law. These fiduciary duties arise by operation of law and attach to any controlling shareholder that exercises actual control over a corporation\'s governance and decision-making.

3\. The duty of loyalty is the most fundamental of these fiduciary obligations. It prohibits a controlling shareholder from using its control over the company to extract value for the controlling shareholder\'s benefit, or for the benefit of its affiliates, at the expense of minority shareholders. A controlling shareholder may not stand on both sides of a transaction and use its position of power to favor itself or a related entity to the detriment of the minority.

4\. Whitmore Capital Partners LLC breached its duty of loyalty by directing Cascade to license its most valuable asset --- the ThermalCore technology portfolio, embodied in fourteen United States patents and associated proprietary know-how --- to Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC, a company controlled by Whitmore through a different fund vehicle (Whitmore Fund II LP), at an annual license fee of \$2.8 million. This rate is substantially below the independently determined fair market value of \$9.5 million per year, representing a discount of more than 70% from fair market rates.

5\. Whitmore\'s breach of the duty of loyalty is further demonstrated by the following conduct:

> a\. Whitmore failed to obtain or commission an independent valuation of the ThermalCore technology license before approving the License Agreement, despite the obvious conflict of interest and despite Ms. Okafor\'s specific demand that an independent valuation be obtained;
>
> b\. Whitmore overrode the written objection of minority shareholder representative Janet Okafor, who specifically identified the self-dealing nature of the transaction and the conflict inherent in Whitmore Capital Partners\' management of both the fund controlling the licensor and the fund owning the licensee;
>
> c\. Whitmore failed to constitute a committee of disinterested directors to evaluate and approve the License Agreement, instead relying on the votes of Whitmore-appointed board members who owed their positions to the controlling shareholder;
>
> d\. Whitmore structured the transaction to benefit Whitmore Fund II LP and its portfolio company Volaris at the direct expense of Cascade and its minority shareholders, effectively transferring \$46.9 million in value from a Fund III portfolio company to a Fund II portfolio company under Whitmore Capital Partners\' common management and control.

6\. Because the License Agreement is a transaction between a controlling shareholder and an entity affiliated with and controlled by that same controlling shareholder through a common manager, the entire fairness standard of review applies under Delaware law. Under the entire fairness doctrine, Whitmore bears the burden of demonstrating that the challenged transaction was the product of both fair dealing and fair price. *See Weinberger v. UOP, Inc.*, 457 A.2d 701 (Del. 1983).

7\. Fair dealing requires that the transaction be negotiated, structured, and approved through a process that is free from overreaching and that provides the minority with meaningful protections. No such process occurred here. Fair price requires that the terms of the transaction fall within a range of reasonableness. A license fee set at less than one-third of fair market value is manifestly not a fair price.

8\. As a direct and proximate result of Whitmore\'s breach of the duty of loyalty, Cascade and its minority shareholders have suffered damages of not less than \$46.9 million.

**B. Count II --- Self-Dealing Transaction in Violation of Operating Agreement**

9\. Claimants incorporate by reference all preceding paragraphs of this Demand as though set forth fully herein.

10\. Section 8.7 of the Amended and Restated Operating Agreement of Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. governs transactions between the Company and affiliates of any shareholder holding 50% or more of the Company\'s outstanding shares. Section 8.7 provides, in pertinent part, that any such affiliate transaction must satisfy two cumulative requirements: (a) the transaction must be approved by a majority of disinterested directors of the Company; and (b) the transaction must be on terms no less favorable to the Company than those that would be available in an arm\'s-length transaction with an unrelated third party.

11\. Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC is an \"affiliate\" of Whitmore within the meaning of the Operating Agreement\'s defined terms. Both Volaris and the entity holding 62% of Cascade (Whitmore Fund III LP) are controlled by the same ultimate parent: Whitmore Capital Partners LLC, which serves as general partner and investment manager of both Whitmore Fund II LP (which owns Volaris) and Whitmore Fund III LP (which owns 62% of Cascade). Under the Operating Agreement, an \"affiliate\" includes any entity that directly or indirectly controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with a shareholder or its manager. Volaris is under common control with Whitmore Fund III LP by virtue of Whitmore Capital Partners\' management of both fund vehicles.

12\. The License Agreement was not approved by a majority of disinterested directors. The only board member who could arguably be considered disinterested --- Janet Okafor, the minority shareholder representative --- voted against the License Agreement and submitted a written objection. The board members who approved the License Agreement were appointed by Whitmore Fund III LP and were not disinterested with respect to a transaction benefiting another Whitmore-managed fund\'s portfolio company. The first prong of Section 8.7 was not satisfied.

13\. The License Agreement is not on terms that are no less favorable to Cascade than those that would be available in an arm\'s-length transaction with an unrelated third party. The Ridgeline Advisory Group independent valuation establishes that the fair market rate for the ThermalCore technology license is \$9.5 million per year. The License Agreement sets the rate at \$2.8 million per year --- less than 30% of fair market value. The second prong of Section 8.7 was not satisfied.

14\. The License Agreement violated both prongs of Section 8.7 of the Operating Agreement. Claimants are entitled to rescission of the License Agreement and damages arising from the breach.

**C. Count III --- Unjust Enrichment**

15\. Claimants incorporate by reference all preceding paragraphs of this Demand as though set forth fully herein.

16\. In the alternative, and to the extent the License Agreement is not rescinded, Whitmore Capital Partners LLC and its affiliates --- including Whitmore Fund II LP and Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC --- have been unjustly enriched by receiving the benefit of the ThermalCore technology license at below-market rates. Volaris has received and continues to receive the full benefit of exclusive access to Cascade\'s most valuable proprietary technology at a cost of \$2.8 million per year, while the fair market value of that access is \$9.5 million per year. The differential of \$6.7 million per year represents a benefit conferred upon Volaris at Cascade\'s expense.

17\. It would be unjust and inequitable to permit Whitmore and its affiliates to retain this benefit, which was obtained not through arm\'s-length commercial bargaining but through the exercise of controlling shareholder power in a self-dealing transaction that was neither properly authorized nor conducted at fair market terms. Equity demands that the unjust enrichment be disgorged.

18\. Claimants seek disgorgement of the benefit conferred, measured by the differential between the license rate and the fair market value for the period the License Agreement has been in effect, plus prospective damages for the remaining term of the agreement, subject to present-value discounting as appropriate.

**D. Count IV --- Waste of Corporate Assets**

19\. Claimants incorporate by reference all preceding paragraphs of this Demand as though set forth fully herein.

20\. The License Agreement constitutes a waste of Cascade\'s corporate assets. The disposal of an exclusive license to Cascade\'s most valuable proprietary technology at a rate of \$2.8 million per year --- when the fair market value is \$9.5 million per year --- is so far below market value that no reasonable business person, acting in good faith and in the interests of the corporation, would approve such a transaction. The terms of the License Agreement are so one-sided that they cannot be explained by any legitimate business purpose, competitive rationale, or strategic justification.

21\. The sole beneficiary of this below-market license is not Cascade, but rather Whitmore Capital Partners LLC\'s other fund vehicle, Whitmore Fund II LP, and its portfolio company, Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC. The transaction was not designed to advance Cascade\'s interests; it was designed to advance the interests of Whitmore Capital Partners\' broader fund management enterprise by enhancing the value and performance of a different fund\'s portfolio company. This constitutes a waste of corporate assets under Delaware law, and Claimants are entitled to relief accordingly.

**[VII. DAMAGES]{.underline}**

**A. Compensatory Damages Calculation**

Claimants seek compensatory damages representing the value diverted from Cascade through the below-market license of ThermalCore technology to Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC. The compensatory damages calculation is straightforward:

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                                                                    **Amount**
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------
  Annual fair market value of ThermalCore license (per Ridgeline Advisory Group)   \$9,500,000

  Annual license fee under the License Agreement                                   \$2,800,000

  Annual damages (differential)                                                    \$6,700,000

  License term                                                                     7 years (March 1, 2023 -- February 28, 2030)

  **Total compensatory damages**                                                   **\$46,900,000**
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

As of the date of this filing (March 15, 2024), the License Agreement has been in effect for approximately 12.5 months. Damages accrued from March 1, 2023 through March 15, 2024 total approximately \$6,979,167 on a prorated basis. Future damages for the remaining approximately 5.95 years of the license term (March 15, 2024 through February 28, 2030) total approximately \$39,865,000, subject to present-value discount at an appropriate rate to be determined at the evidentiary hearing.

These damages represent value diverted from Cascade to Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC, and through Volaris to Whitmore Fund II LP, as a direct result of the below-market license imposed by Whitmore Capital Partners LLC through its control of Cascade\'s Board.

**B. Equitable Relief --- Rescission**

Claimants seek rescission of the Technology License Agreement dated March 1, 2023, between Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. and Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC, in its entirety. The License Agreement was the product of self-dealing by a controlling shareholder, was not approved through the affiliate-transaction procedures required by Section 8.7 of the Operating Agreement, and was executed on terms far below fair market value. The License Agreement was neither fairly negotiated nor fairly priced.

Rescission would restore the status quo ante and prevent ongoing harm to Cascade and its minority shareholders. Upon rescission, Volaris would be required to cease all use of ThermalCore technology, and Cascade would be free to license the technology to any party --- including Volaris, if it so chose --- on arm\'s-length terms reflecting fair market value. Rescission is the most complete remedy for the harm inflicted and is warranted by the circumstances.

**C. Disgorgement of Profits**

In the alternative or in addition to compensatory damages, Claimants seek disgorgement of all profits earned by Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC attributable to its use of ThermalCore technology under the License Agreement. Disgorgement is an appropriate equitable remedy for a controlling shareholder\'s breach of fiduciary duty, particularly where the controlling shareholder\'s affiliate has profited directly from the wrongful conduct. The principle underlying disgorgement is that a fiduciary who profits from a breach of duty should not be permitted to retain the fruits of its wrongdoing.

Claimants reserve the right to present expert testimony at the evidentiary hearing quantifying Volaris\'s profits attributable to the licensed technology. The disgorgement amount may exceed the compensatory damages figure of \$46.9 million to the extent that Volaris\'s profits from exploiting ThermalCore technology exceed the license fee differential.

**D. Attorneys\' Fees and Costs**

Claimants seek an award of reasonable attorneys\' fees, costs, and expenses incurred in prosecuting this arbitration. Under Delaware law, a successful derivative claim may support an award of fees to the derivative plaintiffs\' counsel where the litigation confers a benefit on the corporation. *See, e.g., Sugarland Industries, Inc. v. Thomas*, 420 A.2d 142 (Del. 1980). In addition, Claimants seek reimbursement of the costs incurred in retaining Ridgeline Advisory Group to perform the independent valuation of the ThermalCore technology license, the amount of which will be proved at the evidentiary hearing. Claimants further request that all arbitration costs, including the arbitrator\'s fees and AAA administrative fees, be allocated to Respondent.

**[VIII. IDENTIFICATION OF ANTICIPATED WITNESSES]{.underline}**

**A. Fact Witnesses**

Claimants anticipate presenting testimony from the following fact witnesses at the evidentiary hearing:

1\. **Janet Okafor** --- Member of the Board of Directors of Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. and minority shareholder holding 11.4% of the Company\'s outstanding shares. Ms. Okafor will testify regarding the Board\'s deliberations concerning the License Agreement, including the circumstances under which the Whitmore-controlled board majority approved the transaction; her written objection dated February 15, 2023; her demand for an independent valuation; Whitmore\'s exercise of control over the Board\'s decision-making process; and the failure of the Board to follow the affiliate-transaction procedures set forth in Section 8.7 of the Operating Agreement.

2\. **Garrett T. Whitmore** --- Founder and Managing Partner of Whitmore Capital Partners LLC. Mr. Whitmore is anticipated to be called as an adverse witness. He is expected to testify regarding his personal direction of the License Agreement negotiations and terms; his control over both Whitmore Fund II LP (which owns Volaris) and Whitmore Fund III LP (which controls Cascade); the investment strategy and rationale underlying the cross-fund transaction; and the business justification --- or lack thereof --- for setting the license rate at \$2.8 million per year.

3\. **Thomas Kinnear** --- Chief Executive Officer of Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC. Mr. Kinnear is anticipated to be called as an adverse or non-party witness. He is expected to testify regarding the negotiation process for the License Agreement; the direction he received from Whitmore Capital Partners in connection with the transaction; Volaris\'s use of ThermalCore technology in its operations; and the revenues and profits Volaris has derived from its access to the licensed technology.

4\. **Additional Cascade Board Members** --- Other members of the Cascade Board of Directors may be called to testify regarding the approval process, their understanding of the license terms, their awareness of the conflict of interest, and the basis for their votes in favor of the License Agreement.

**B. Expert Witnesses**

Claimants anticipate presenting testimony from the following expert witnesses at the evidentiary hearing:

1\. **Valuation Expert, Ridgeline Advisory Group** (name to be formally designated in expert disclosures). This witness will testify regarding the independent fair market valuation of the ThermalCore technology license, including the methodology employed (comparable transactions, discounted cash flow, and market analysis), the data and assumptions underlying the valuation, and the conclusion that the fair market value of the license is \$9.5 million per year. The Ridgeline expert will further address the reasonableness of the \$2.8 million per year license fee in comparison to comparable arm\'s-length license transactions in the industrial heat-exchange sector.

2\. **Dr. Rita Peshkov, Vice President of Research & Development, Cascade Thermal Systems Inc.** Dr. Peshkov is anticipated to serve as Claimants\' key expert witness on technical matters relating to the ThermalCore technology portfolio. Dr. Peshkov is the Company\'s foremost authority on ThermalCore technology and the named inventor or co-inventor on the majority of Cascade\'s fourteen patents. She will provide expert testimony regarding: (a) the technical specifications, functionality, and uniqueness of the ThermalCore technology portfolio, including the advanced phase-change heat-exchange systems and integrated thermal management platforms covered by the patents; (b) the development history of the fourteen patents and the proprietary know-how embodied in the licensed technology, including the years of research and development investment required to bring the technology to its current state; (c) the competitive advantages conferred upon any licensee of ThermalCore technology, including enhanced operational efficiency, energy savings, and improved process reliability; (d) the absence of commercially viable substitute technologies in the industrial heat-exchange market, which supports the premium value of an exclusive ThermalCore license; and (e) her expert opinion on the technical basis for the valuation methodology employed by Ridgeline Advisory Group, including the appropriateness of the comparable transactions selected and the revenue assumptions underlying the discounted cash flow analysis.

Dr. Peshkov holds a Ph.D. in mechanical engineering and has published extensively in the field of advanced thermal engineering, including in peer-reviewed journals such as the *Journal of Advanced Thermal Engineering*. She has presented her research at numerous industry conferences and is recognized as a leading authority in the design and commercialization of heat-exchange technology for industrial applications. Her expertise is essential to the Tribunal\'s understanding of the value and irreplaceability of the ThermalCore technology that is the subject of the challenged License Agreement. Dr. Peshkov\'s curriculum vitae, which details her full educational background, professional experience, patent inventorship, and publication history, is referenced as Exhibit H to this Demand.

3\. **Damages and Financial Expert** (name to be formally designated in expert disclosures). This expert will testify regarding the calculation of compensatory damages, present-value discounting of future damages, disgorgement calculations, and the financial impact of the License Agreement on Cascade\'s enterprise value and the minority shareholders\' equity interests.

**[IX. ARBITRATION CLAUSE AND PROCEDURAL MATTERS]{.underline}**

This arbitration is administered under the AAA Commercial Arbitration Rules, as mandated by Section 12.3 of the Operating Agreement. The mandatory arbitration clause requires a single arbitrator selected from the AAA\'s panel of commercial arbitrators in accordance with the AAA\'s standard arbitrator selection procedures.

The seat of arbitration is Minneapolis, Minnesota, as specified in the Operating Agreement. The governing substantive law is the law of the State of Delaware. The Federal Arbitration Act, 9 U.S.C. §§ 1--16, governs the enforceability and procedural aspects of the arbitration agreement.

The amount in controversy in this arbitration is \$46.9 million in compensatory damages alone, exclusive of claims for disgorgement, equitable relief, attorneys\' fees, and costs. This amount qualifies this matter for administration under the AAA\'s Large Complex Case Supplementary Procedures. Claimants respectfully request that the AAA administer this matter under its Large Complex Case Supplementary Procedures in addition to the standard Commercial Arbitration Rules, given the magnitude and complexity of the claims, the number and nature of anticipated witnesses, and the volume of documentary evidence.

Claimants seek a preliminary hearing within ninety (90) days of the appointment of the arbitrator to establish a comprehensive scheduling order. The scheduling order should address, at a minimum: dates for initial document exchange and production; a schedule for depositions of fact and expert witnesses; deadlines for expert disclosures, including written expert reports; a date for the exchange of pre-hearing briefs; and a date for the evidentiary hearing. Claimants further note that the derivative nature of certain claims asserted in this proceeding may require the appointment of a guardian ad litem or the implementation of special procedures to protect the interests of the Company. Claimants reserve the right to address these procedural matters at the preliminary hearing.

**[X. PRAYER FOR RELIEF]{.underline}**

**WHEREFORE**, Claimants respectfully request that the Arbitral Tribunal enter an award in favor of Claimants and against Respondent Whitmore Capital Partners LLC as follows:

1\. **Compensatory damages** in the amount of Forty-Six Million Nine Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$46,900,000.00), or such greater or lesser amount as may be proved at the evidentiary hearing, representing the total value diverted from Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. through the below-market Technology License Agreement with Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC over the seven-year license term;

2\. **Rescission** of the Technology License Agreement dated March 1, 2023, between Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. and Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC, in its entirety;

3\. **Disgorgement** of all profits earned by Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC attributable to its use of ThermalCore technology under the License Agreement, in an amount to be determined at the evidentiary hearing;

4\. **A declaration** that Whitmore Capital Partners LLC breached its fiduciary duties of loyalty and care owed to Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. and its minority shareholders by directing the Company to enter into the License Agreement on terms substantially below fair market value with an entity controlled by Whitmore through a separate fund vehicle;

5\. **A declaration** that the License Agreement constitutes a self-dealing transaction that violated Section 8.7 of the Amended and Restated Operating Agreement of Cascade Thermal Systems Inc.;

6\. **Pre-award and post-award interest** at the maximum rate permitted by applicable law;

7\. **An award of Claimants\' reasonable attorneys\' fees, costs, and expenses**, including the cost of the independent valuation performed by Ridgeline Advisory Group;

8\. **Allocation of all arbitration costs**, including arbitrator fees and AAA administrative fees, to Respondent; and

9\. **Such other and further relief** as the Tribunal deems just, equitable, and appropriate under the circumstances.

**(a)** The parties shall be deemed to have made these Rules a part of their arbitration agreement whenever they have provided for arbitration by the American Arbitration Association under its Commercial Arbitration Rules or have otherwise agreed to arbitration administered by the AAA in a commercial dispute. **(b)** The parties may, by mutual written agreement, modify any provision of these Rules, except where these Rules expressly provide that a particular provision may not be modified by the parties. Any such modification shall be communicated in writing to the AAA and to the arbitrator.

**(c)** The AAA shall administer the arbitration in accordance with these Rules. These Rules shall be interpreted and applied by the AAA and by the arbitrator in a manner consistent with their purpose of providing a fair, efficient, and economical process for the resolution of disputes.

**[Rule R-12. Appointment from National Roster]{.underline}**

**(a)** If the parties have not agreed upon an arbitrator or a method for appointing an arbitrator, the AAA shall send simultaneously to each party an identical list of not fewer than five (5) names of persons drawn from the AAA National Roster of Arbitrators. Each name on the list shall be accompanied by a brief biographical summary and any disclosures then available.

**(b)** The parties may agree upon an arbitrator from the submitted list. If the parties are unable to agree upon an arbitrator within the time period specified by the AAA, each party shall strike from the list the names of any persons to whom that party objects, shall number the remaining names in order of preference, and shall return the list to the AAA within the time period specified.

**(c)** From among the persons who have been approved on both lists, and in accordance with the order of mutual preference as indicated by the parties, the AAA shall invite the acceptance of an arbitrator to serve. If no mutually acceptable arbitrator is identified through this process, or if the identified arbitrator is unable or unwilling to serve, the AAA shall have the authority to appoint an arbitrator from the National Roster without further submission of lists to the parties.

**(d)** The AAA shall notify all parties of the appointment of the arbitrator and shall confirm the appointment in writing.

**[Rule R-16. Disclosure and Challenge Procedure --- General]{.underline}**

Any person appointed or to be appointed as an arbitrator shall disclose to the AAA any circumstance likely to give rise to justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator\'s impartiality or independence, including any bias or any financial or personal interest in the result of the arbitration, or any past or present relationship with the parties, their representatives, or their counsel. The obligation to disclose under this Rule and Rule R-17 shall be construed broadly to further the goal of ensuring that arbitrators are and remain impartial and independent. The standard of \"justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator\'s impartiality or independence\" is intended to encompass all circumstances that a reasonable person would consider relevant in evaluating an arbitrator\'s ability to render a fair and unbiased determination.

**[Rule R-17. Disclosure]{.underline}**

**(a)** A person appointed as an arbitrator shall disclose to the AAA any circumstance likely to give rise to justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator\'s impartiality or independence. The obligation of disclosure extends to, but is not limited to, the following categories of information:

> **(i)** Any known direct or indirect financial or personal interest in the outcome of the arbitration, including any interest, whether legal or beneficial, in the subject matter of the dispute, in any party, or in any entity that may be affected by the result of the proceeding;
>
> **(ii)** Any known existing or past financial, business, professional, family, or social relationships with any party to the arbitration, its counsel, representatives, or witnesses that are likely to affect impartiality or that might reasonably create an appearance of partiality or bias. This includes, without limitation, relationships arising from employment, professional association, joint ventures, board memberships, academic collaboration, or regular social contact;
>
> **(iii)** Any prior or pending cases in which the arbitrator has been or is involved as arbitrator, counsel, expert, or in any other capacity, with any party to the arbitration, its counsel, or representatives. This obligation extends to all matters, whether administered by the AAA or by any other arbitral institution, and includes matters in which the arbitrator served in any neutral or non-neutral capacity;
>
> **(iv)** Any other circumstances that might cause a reasonable party to question the arbitrator\'s ability to render a fair and impartial determination. This catch-all provision is intended to ensure comprehensive disclosure and shall be interpreted broadly to encompass facts and circumstances not specifically enumerated in subparagraphs (i) through (iii) above, including but not limited to the arbitrator\'s prior publications, public statements, or expressed views on legal or factual issues relevant to the dispute.

**(b)** The obligation of disclosure is a continuing obligation throughout the arbitration proceeding. An arbitrator must disclose any new information or any change in circumstances that arises after the initial disclosure as soon as practicable. The arbitrator shall not await inquiry by a party or by the AAA before making any required supplemental disclosure.

**(c)** Upon receipt of disclosures from an arbitrator, the AAA shall communicate the disclosures to the parties promptly and in their entirety. If the arbitrator, at any stage during the arbitration, becomes aware of new circumstances that should be disclosed, the arbitrator shall promptly disclose such circumstances to the parties and to the AAA. The AAA shall transmit any supplemental disclosures to all parties within two (2) business days of receipt.

**(d)** The failure of an arbitrator to make complete and timely disclosures as required by this Rule may result in the arbitrator\'s removal in accordance with Rule R-18, and may constitute grounds for vacatur of any resulting award under applicable law, including but not limited to Section 10 of the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. § 10). The AAA may, in its discretion, bring any disclosure deficiency to the attention of the arbitrator and request supplemental disclosure before acting on any challenge.

**[Rule R-18. Disqualification of Arbitrator; Challenge Procedure]{.underline}**

**(a)** Any party may challenge the appointment of an arbitrator on grounds that circumstances exist that give rise to justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator\'s impartiality or independence. A challenge must be made by written notice to the AAA and to the other party, stating with specificity the grounds for the challenge, within **seven (7) calendar days** after (i) the appointment of the challenged arbitrator has been communicated to the challenging party, or (ii) the circumstances giving rise to the challenge became known or should reasonably have become known to the challenging party, whichever is later.

**(b)** Upon receipt of a challenge, the AAA shall communicate the challenge to the other party or parties, who may respond in writing within seven (7) calendar days of receipt of the challenge. The challenged arbitrator shall also be notified and may submit a written response to the AAA within the same period. The AAA may, in its discretion, extend the response period for good cause shown.

**(c)** If the other party agrees to the challenge, or the challenged arbitrator voluntarily withdraws from the proceeding, a replacement arbitrator shall be appointed in accordance with Rule R-19. The withdrawal of an arbitrator or the agreement of a party to a challenge shall not imply acceptance of the validity of the grounds stated in the challenge.

**(d)** If the other party does not agree to the challenge, and the challenged arbitrator does not voluntarily withdraw, the AAA shall make the decision on the challenge. In deciding the challenge, the AAA shall consider the circumstances disclosed by the arbitrator, the disclosures required but not made, the grounds stated in the challenge, any responses received from the opposing party and the challenged arbitrator, and any other relevant information available to the AAA. The AAA may conduct such inquiry as it deems appropriate before rendering its decision.

**(e)** The decision of the AAA on a challenge is administrative in nature and shall not be subject to appeal within the AAA\'s administrative process. However, a party\'s right to challenge an arbitrator or to seek vacatur of an award under Section 10 of the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. § 10) or applicable state law is not affected by this Rule. Nothing in this Rule shall be construed to limit any right that a party may have under applicable law to seek judicial review of an award on the grounds of evident partiality or other statutory bases for vacatur.

**(f)** A party that fails to make a timely challenge under this Rule shall be deemed to have waived its right to challenge the arbitrator on the grounds then known to the party, except that a party shall not be deemed to have waived its right to challenge based on grounds that were not and could not reasonably have been known at the time of the deadline established by subparagraph (a). Nothing in this Rule shall preclude a party from raising issues of evident partiality in a proceeding to confirm, modify, or vacate an award under applicable law, provided that such issues were not previously known and subject to waiver under this subparagraph.

**[Rule R-19. Replacement of Arbitrator]{.underline}**

**(a)** If an arbitrator is removed pursuant to Rule R-18, withdraws, becomes unable or unwilling to serve, or is otherwise disqualified, the AAA shall appoint a replacement arbitrator in accordance with the procedures set forth in Rule R-12, unless the parties agree upon an alternative method for selecting a replacement arbitrator.

**(b)** The replacement arbitrator shall determine, in his or her sole discretion after consulting the parties, whether any portion of the prior proceedings shall be repeated. In making this determination, the replacement arbitrator shall consider the stage of the proceedings, the nature of the prior proceedings, and the interests of the parties in an efficient resolution of the dispute.

**(c)** In the event a replacement arbitrator is appointed, the time limits and deadlines established in these Rules or by prior order of the original arbitrator shall be adjusted as appropriate by the AAA in consultation with the replacement arbitrator and the parties.

**[Rule R-20. Communication with Arbitrator]{.underline}**

**(a)** No party or party representative shall communicate ex parte with the arbitrator regarding any substantive matter related to the arbitration. Parties may communicate with the arbitrator regarding purely administrative and scheduling matters, provided that notice of any such communication is given simultaneously to all other parties.

**(b)** Any written communications sent to the arbitrator by a party shall be simultaneously provided to the other party or parties. The arbitrator shall not consider any written communication from a party that has not been shared with all other parties to the proceeding.

**[Rule R-24. Preliminary Hearing]{.underline}**

At the discretion of the arbitrator or at the request of any party, a preliminary hearing shall be scheduled and held as soon as practicable after the appointment of the arbitrator. The preliminary hearing may address procedural matters including, but not limited to, the exchange of information, identification of issues, stipulations of fact or law, scheduling of hearings and submissions, and any other matters that will expedite the arbitration proceedings. The arbitrator may issue a scheduling order following the preliminary hearing.

**[Rule R-35. Evidence]{.underline}**

**(a)** The arbitrator shall be the judge of the relevance and materiality of the evidence offered, and conformity to legal rules of evidence shall not be necessary. The arbitrator may, in his or her discretion, order the production of documents and other information and may order the parties to furnish such additional evidence as the arbitrator deems necessary to an understanding and determination of the dispute.

**(b)** The arbitrator shall give the parties adequate opportunity to present evidence and argument in support of their respective positions. The arbitrator may receive and consider the evidence of witnesses by declaration or affidavit, but shall give appropriate weight to any objections made to the admissibility of such evidence.

**[Rule R-48. Serving of Notice and Communications]{.underline}**

Notices and communications under these Rules may be served by personal delivery, registered or certified mail, commercial overnight delivery service, email, or other means of written communication reasonably calculated to ensure receipt. When serving notices regarding a challenge under Rule R-18, service must be made by means reasonably calculated to ensure timely receipt by the AAA and by the other party within the time period specified by that Rule.

**Editor\'s Note:** These excerpts are compiled from the AAA Commercial Arbitration Rules and Mediation Procedures as currently in effect. For the complete text of the Rules, parties should consult the American Arbitration Association\'s official publications, available at www.adr.org. Additionally, parties and arbitrators should be aware that obligations of disclosure and standards of impartiality and independence are also addressed in the following authorities, which may be applicable to proceedings under these Rules:

> • The **Code of Ethics for Arbitrators in Commercial Disputes** (originally prepared by a joint committee of the American Arbitration Association and the American Bar Association, most recently amended effective March 1, 2004), including Canon I (upholding the integrity and fairness of the arbitration process), Canon II (disclosure obligations), and Canon IV (conducting the proceedings fairly and diligently);
>
> • The **IBA Guidelines on Conflicts of Interest in International Arbitration** (adopted by resolution of the IBA Council on October 23, 2014), including the General Standards regarding disclosure and the Non-Waivable Red List, Waivable Red List, Orange List, and Green List categorizations;
>
> • Applicable provisions of the **Federal Arbitration Act**, 9 U.S.C. §§ 1--16, including Section 10(a)(2) (providing for vacatur of an award \"where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them\").

Parties are encouraged to review these additional authorities in conjunction with these Rules when evaluating disclosure adequacy and arbitrator impartiality.
