---

## I. Executive Summary

This memorandum assesses whether the June 17, 2024 Arbitrator Disclosure Statement of Hon. Margaret R. Fairhaven (Ret.) ("the Arbitrator") provides a basis for a challenge under AAA Commercial Arbitration Rules R-16 through R-18, the AAA/ABA Code of Ethics for Arbitrators in Commercial Disputes, the IBA Guidelines on Conflicts of Interest in International Arbitration, and Section 10(a)(2) of the Federal Arbitration Act. It is based on a comparison of the Disclosure Statement against the Arbitration Demand, the AAA rules excerpts, the compiled arbitrator appointment data, and the entity and relationship profiles prepared from public records.

**Bottom line.** The Disclosure Statement is materially deficient. Cross-referencing the Arbitrator's disclosures against the public-record research identifies **five categories of relationship or interest that were not disclosed**, an **incomplete account of the Arbitrator's prior and pending appointments** involving the parties' counsel, and an **incomplete description of the Arbitrator's institutional affiliation** with Lakeview Dispute Resolution Center. Several of these omissions fall squarely within categories that AAA Rule R-17 expressly requires be disclosed, and the Arbitrator's certification that her statement "represents all circumstances known to me" is, on the present record, inaccurate.

The undisclosed matters are not uniform in their strategic direction:

- **One undisclosed connection favors a challenge by the Respondent (Whitmore):** the co-authorship of a peer-reviewed article by the Arbitrator's spouse and the Claimant's anticipated key expert witness, Dr. Rita Peshkov, on a topic that is the technical core of this arbitration. This is the cleanest single ground available to our client.
- **Four undisclosed connections, together with the repeat-appointment pattern, favor a challenge by the Claimant (Cascade):** the Arbitrator's possible indirect financial interest in Whitmore Fund II LP (the fund that owns the licensee Volaris); the shared organizational service between the Arbitrator and Respondent's principal Garrett T. Whitmore at the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute; the co-panelist relationship between the Arbitrator and lead Respondent's counsel Dominic Hargrave at a CLE on a directly germane topic; and the omission of a pending arbitration in which Barrow & Thistlewood LLP appears.
- **The repeat-appointment and fee-concentration data** (Barrow & Thistlewood LLP appeared in 45% of the Arbitrator's last 20 completed arbitrations and generated an estimated 48% of her arbitration income, with the firm's clients prevailing in 7 of 9 completed matters) bear on both sides' evaluation and on the risk of vacatur.

**Deadline.** Under Rule R-18(a), any challenge must be filed in writing with the AAA and served on opposing counsel within **seven (7) calendar days of the disclosure — i.e., by June 24, 2024**. Because the deficiencies identified below were not disclosed and could not reasonably have been known to the parties from the Disclosure Statement itself, Rule R-18(f) preserves the right to raise them notwithstanding the deadline, and the right to assert evident partiality in any later vacatur proceeding under FAA § 10 is unaffected. Nonetheless, we recommend acting within the June 24 window to preserve all procedural options.

**Recommendation.** We recommend (1) promptly requesting a supplemental disclosure from the Arbitrator under Rule R-17(d) directed at each of the items in Part IV; (2) preserving our client's right to challenge on the Peshkov co-authorship ground by the June 24 deadline if the supplemental disclosure does not resolve the concern; and (3) preparing for the strong likelihood that Claimant's counsel will independently identify and challenge on the connections between the Arbitrator and the Respondent. The vacatur exposure under FAA § 10(a)(2) is material and should be weighed before any party elects to proceed with this Arbitrator.

---

## II. Procedural Framework and Governing Standards

### A. The AAA disclosure and challenge rules

**Rule R-16** establishes the governing standard: an arbitrator "shall disclose to the AAA any circumstance likely to give rise to justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator's impartiality or independence, including any bias or any financial or personal interest in the result of the arbitration, or any past or present relationship with the parties, their representatives, or their counsel." The Rule directs that the obligation "shall be construed broadly to further the goal of ensuring that arbitrators are and remain impartial and independent," and that the standard of "justifiable doubt" is "intended to encompass all circumstances that a reasonable person would consider relevant."

**Rule R-17(a)** enumerates four non-exclusive categories of required disclosure:

- **(a)(i)** — "Any known direct or indirect financial or personal interest in the outcome of the arbitration, including any interest, whether legal or beneficial, in the subject matter of the dispute, in any party, or in any entity that may be affected by the result of the proceeding";
- **(a)(ii)** — "Any known existing or past financial, business, professional, family, or social relationships with any party to the arbitration, its counsel, representatives, or witnesses that are likely to affect impartiality or that might reasonably create an appearance of partiality or bias," expressly "including ... relationships arising from employment, professional association, joint ventures, board memberships, academic collaboration, or regular social contact";
- **(a)(iii)** — "Any prior or pending cases in which the arbitrator has been or is involved ... with any party to the arbitration, its counsel, or representatives," extending to all matters regardless of institution or capacity; and
- **(a)(iv)** — a catch-all covering "any other circumstances that might cause a reasonable party to question the arbitrator's ability to render a fair and impartial determination," expressly "including but not limited to the arbitrator's prior publications, public statements, or expressed views on legal or factual issues relevant to the dispute."

**Rule R-17(b)** makes disclosure a **continuing obligation**; the arbitrator "shall not await inquiry by a party or by the AAA before making any required supplemental disclosure." **Rule R-17(d)** provides that the "failure of an arbitrator to make complete and timely disclosures ... may result in the arbitrator's removal in accordance with Rule R-18, and may constitute grounds for vacatur of any resulting award under applicable law, including but not limited to Section 10 of the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. § 10)."

**Rule R-18(a)** requires that any challenge be made "by written notice to the AAA and to the other party, stating with specificity the grounds for the challenge, within seven (7) calendar days after (i) the appointment of the challenged arbitrator has been communicated to the challenging party, or (ii) the circumstances giving rise to the challenge became known or should reasonably have become known to the challenging party, whichever is later." **Rule R-18(f)** provides that a party that fails to timely challenge "shall be deemed to have waived its right to challenge the arbitrator on the grounds then known to the party, except that a party shall not be deemed to have waived its right to challenge based on grounds that were not and could not reasonably have been known at the time of the deadline," and expressly preserves the right to seek vacatur on grounds of evident partiality "provided that such issues were not previously known and subject to waiver."

### B. The FAA and the "evident partiality" standard

Section 10(a)(2) of the Federal Arbitration Act, 9 U.S.C. § 10(a)(2), permits vacatur of an award "where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators." The Supreme Court in *Commonwealth Coatings Corp. v. Continental Casualty Co.*, 393 U.S. 145 (1968), held that arbitrators must "avoid the appearance of partiality" and disclose "any dealings that might create an impression of possible bias," recognizing that professional arbitrators with ongoing business relationships must nevertheless disclose connections that a reasonable party would want to know. The Second Circuit's decision in *Morelite Construction Corp. v. New York City District Council Carpenters Pension Funds*, 952 F.2d 16 (2d Cir. 1991), found evident partiality where an arbitrator's employer had a financial relationship with a party — illustrating that **economic dependence** on a party (or, by extension, on a firm that regularly appears before the arbitrator) can supply evident partiality even without proof of actual bias.

### C. The Code of Ethics and the IBA Guidelines

The AAA rules excerpts direct attention to the **AAA/ABA Code of Ethics for Arbitrators in Commercial Disputes** (Canon I — integrity and fairness; Canon II — disclosure; Canon IV — fair conduct) and the **IBA Guidelines on Conflicts of Interest in International Arbitration**. The IBA Guidelines' General Standards require disclosure of any fact that may, from a reasonable third party's perspective, give rise to doubts as to impartiality or independence, and categorize conflicts into a Non-Waivable Red List, a Waivable Red List, an Orange List (disclose; waivable if no objection), and a Green List (no disclosure needed). As discussed below, several of the undisclosed items here fall at least within Orange List territory (shared board/committee service, prior appointments, prior professional interaction with counsel), and the indirect financial interest approaches Red List treatment.

---

## III. Factual Background

The arbitration arises from a derivative and direct action by the minority shareholders of Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. ("Cascade") against Whitmore Capital Partners LLC ("Whitmore"), the manager of Whitmore Fund III LP, which holds a 62% controlling stake in Cascade. Claimants allege that Whitmore directed Cascade to license its proprietary "ThermalCore" heat-exchange technology to Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC ("Volaris") — a portfolio company of a *different* Whitmore-managed fund, Whitmore Fund II LP — at \$2.8 million per year, against an independently determined fair market value of \$9.5 million per year, yielding claimed damages of \$46.9 million over the seven-year license term. The claims sound in breach of fiduciary duty, self-dealing in violation of the Cascade Operating Agreement, unjust enrichment, and waste.

The Claimant is represented by **Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP** (lead counsel Priya Narayanan). The Respondent is represented by **Barrow & Thistlewood LLP** (lead partner Dominic Hargrave; Ellen Wu heads the firm's national arbitration practice and is involved in arbitrator-selection strategy for the matter). The Respondent's principal, **Garrett T. Whitmore**, personally directed the challenged transaction and is an anticipated fact witness. The Claimant's anticipated key expert witness is **Dr. Rita Peshkov**, Cascade's Vice President of R&D and the named inventor on most of the ThermalCore patents. Volaris (and its CEO Thomas Kinnear) is a non-party beneficiary of the disputed license and an anticipated non-party witness.

The Arbitrator was selected on June 3, 2024. During the ranking process, **Whitmore ranked the Arbitrator first** and Cascade ranked her third. The AAA confirmed the appointment on June 10, 2024, and the Arbitrator submitted her Disclosure Statement on June 17, 2024.

---

## IV. Assessment of Specific Grounds for Challenge

The following assessment addresses each potential ground, identifying the factual basis, the disclosure status, the applicable rule, the strength of the challenge, and the party for whom the ground is most salient.

### A. Undisclosed indirect financial interest in Whitmore Fund II LP (via the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund)

**Facts.** The Arbitrator discloses that her investment portfolio is managed on a discretionary basis by Northbridge Wealth Advisors and states that she "hold[s] no direct equity positions in any party to this arbitration." She further states that she "ha[s] not undertaken a position-by-position review of each fund or sub-investment held within [her] managed accounts." Public SEC filings and fund disclosure documents show that Northbridge Wealth Advisors also manages the **Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund**, a fund-of-funds vehicle whose most recent public filing (March 2024) reports a **2.3% limited partner interest in Whitmore Fund II LP**. Whitmore Fund II LP owns Volaris, the direct beneficiary of the disputed below-market license. The economic outcome of this arbitration — any award rescinding, renegotiating, or repricing the Volaris license, or imposing damages on Whitmore — would affect the value of Whitmore Fund II LP and, derivatively, the value of a limited partner interest in that fund.

**Disclosure status.** Not disclosed. The Arbitrator's "no direct equity positions" formulation is literally accurate but does not address **indirect** interests, which Rule R-17(a)(i) expressly reaches ("any ... direct or indirect financial ... interest ... in any party, or in any entity that may be affected by the result"). The Arbitrator identifies her manager (Northbridge) but does not identify the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund, its holdings, or the potential chain of indirect exposure to a fund managed by the Respondent.

**Analysis.** Two features are significant. First, the duty under R-17(a)(i) extends to indirect interests and to "any entity that may be affected by the result"; Whitmore Fund II LP is precisely such an entity, because it owns the licensee whose license is the subject of the dispute. Second, the Arbitrator's reliance on discretionary management and her statement that she has not reviewed her holdings do not discharge the disclosure obligation — particularly where the very firm managing her portfolio offers a product invested in a fund managed by the Respondent, a fact that a diligent inquiry (which the Arbitrator certifies she undertook) should have surfaced. Whether the Arbitrator is in fact allocated to the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund is not confirmed by the public record; the point for challenge purposes is that the potential indirect interest was neither investigated nor disclosed, and the "no direct equity positions" language is susceptible to being read as misleading by omission. Under the IBA Guidelines, an interest in an entity affected by the result is at least an Orange List (and arguably Waivable Red List) matter requiring disclosure.

**Strength: Moderate to strong** (contingent on confirming the Arbitrator's allocation to the Select Alternatives Fund; the disclosure deficiency itself is clear). **Favors a challenge by the Claimant**, because the interest is economically aligned with the Respondent/Fund II side of the transaction.

### B. Undisclosed co-authorship between the Arbitrator's spouse and the Claimant's key expert witness (Dr. Rita Peshkov)

**Facts.** The Arbitrator discloses that her husband, Dr. Alan Fairhaven, is a retired professor of mechanical engineering at the University of Minnesota (faculty 1992–2019, emeritus since), and states that "to the best of my knowledge, he has no financial or professional relationship with Cascade Thermal Systems Inc., Whitmore Capital Partners LLC, or any of their officers, directors, or employees." Academic publication databases show that Dr. Alan Fairhaven **co-authored a peer-reviewed article with Dr. Rita Peshkov** — Cascade's Vice President of R&D and the Claimant's anticipated key expert witness — titled "Optimization of Phase-Change Heat Exchangers in High-Load Industrial Applications," published in the *Journal of Advanced Thermal Engineering*, Vol. 42, Issue 3 (September 2022). The subject matter — phase-change heat exchangers in high-load industrial applications — is the technical core of the ThermalCore technology at issue in this arbitration.

**Disclosure status.** Not disclosed. Moreover, the Arbitrator's affirmative statement that her husband "has no ... professional relationship with Cascade ... or any of their officers, directors, or employees" appears to be **inaccurate**: Dr. Peshkov is an officer and employee of Cascade, and the co-authorship reflects a professional (academic collaboration) relationship. Co-authorship of a peer-reviewed article ordinarily reflects a sustained collaboration — joint research design, data collection, drafting, and revision — and the September 2022 publication indicates a relationship that predated the arbitration and may have continued.

**Analysis.** Rule R-17(a)(ii) requires disclosure of "professional, family, or social relationships with any party ... its counsel, representatives, or **witnesses**," expressly including "academic collaboration." The relationship here runs from the Arbitrator's **spouse** to a **witness**, but the disclosure obligation is construed broadly and plainly reaches family members' professional relationships with witnesses — particularly where the witness is central and the subject matter overlaps. The Arbitrator will be called upon to assess Dr. Peshkov's credibility and the weight of her expert testimony on the very technology that Dr. Peshkov and the Arbitrator's husband jointly researched and published on. The appearance that the Arbitrator may be predisposed to credit (or, at minimum, is not institutionally distant from) that testimony is exactly the kind of "appearance of partiality" Rule R-17(a)(ii) targets. The IBA Guidelines treat a family member's professional relationship with a witness as a disclosable Orange List matter; the subject-matter nexus and the witness's centrality elevate its seriousness.

**Strength: Strong.** This is the single cleanest ground for a challenge by the Respondent: it is a clear, documentable disclosure failure under an expressly enumerated category (R-17(a)(ii)), it implicates the Claimant's key expert, and the Arbitrator's affirmative statement about her husband's lack of professional relationships with Cascade personnel appears to be contradicted by the public record. **Favors a challenge by the Respondent.**

### C. Undisclosed co-panelist relationship with lead Respondent's counsel (Dominic Hargrave) on a directly germane CLE topic

**Facts.** On October 14, 2023 — approximately five months before the arbitration demand and eight months before the Arbitrator's appointment — the Lakeview Dispute Resolution Center hosted a CLE panel titled "Arbitrating Complex IP Licensing Disputes." The listed panelists included the Arbitrator and **Dominic Hargrave**, the lead partner for the Respondent in this arbitration (alongside Professor Naomi Strickland and Marcus DelVecchio). The topic is directly germane to this arbitration, which concerns the valuation and enforceability of a technology license. The event was held at Lakeview, the Arbitrator's own affiliated center.

**Disclosure status.** Not disclosed. The Arbitrator's disclosure addresses prior *arbitrations* in which Barrow & Thistlewood LLP appeared, but does not disclose this co-panelist relationship with Mr. Hargrave specifically.

**Analysis.** Rule R-17(a)(ii) reaches professional relationships with counsel; Rule R-17(a)(iv) reaches "public statements, or expressed views on legal or factual issues relevant to the dispute." Co-panelist service involves shared preparation and interaction, and the panel's topic — arbitrating complex IP licensing disputes — is the subject of this case. Standing alone, a single CLE co-panelist event is a relatively modest professional contact (IBA Orange List). It gains force here from three features: (i) the topic is directly germane; (ii) the event was hosted at the Arbitrator's own institution (Lakeview), suggesting a closer nexus than a neutral venue; and (iii) it was not disclosed. The non-disclosure is the actionable element: a reasonable party would want to know that its arbitrator and opposing lead counsel recently co-presented on the very subject of the dispute.

**Strength: Moderate.** Most powerful in combination with the other connections to the Respondent's counsel (the repeat-appointment pattern, the Lakeview referral relationship, and the shared Institute service). **Favors a challenge by the Claimant.**

### D. Undisclosed shared organizational service with the Respondent's principal (Garrett T. Whitmore) at the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute

**Facts.** The Arbitrator discloses that she has served on the **advisory board** of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute since 2021, and that **Ellen Wu** of Barrow & Thistlewood LLP also serves on that advisory board. The Arbitrator does **not** disclose that **Garrett T. Whitmore** — the Respondent's founder, Managing Partner, and the individual who personally directed the challenged transaction and is an anticipated witness — serves on the **Board of Trustees** of the same Institute, as shown by the Institute's IRS Form 990 for fiscal year 2023. The Board of Trustees is the Institute's governing body, responsible for strategic direction, budgetary oversight, and appointments to leadership positions, including the advisory board on which the Arbitrator sits. The Institute is described as a small nonprofit; its bylaws and posted minutes indicate that the Board of Trustees and the advisory board hold annual meetings and periodic joint sessions.

**Disclosure status.** Not disclosed. Notably, the Arbitrator disclosed the Institute affiliation and Ms. Wu's advisory board service, but omitted the Respondent's principal's role on the governing body of the same organization.

**Analysis.** Rule R-17(a)(ii) requires disclosure of professional relationships with a party (or party representative) arising from "board memberships." The Arbitrator and the Respondent's principal serve the same small nonprofit in overlapping governance roles, with the Trustees body formally superior to (and appointing) the advisory board on which the Arbitrator serves. The connection is somewhat attenuated — shared nonprofit board service is common and, disclosed, would often be waivable — but the **failure to disclose** is the problem, particularly given that the Arbitrator opened the topic by disclosing the Institute affiliation and Ms. Wu's role yet omitted the party principal's trusteeship. A reasonable party would consider the Respondent's principal's governing role at the Arbitrator's organization relevant to evaluating the Arbitrator's independence. Under the IBA Guidelines, shared committee/board service in a small organization is an Orange List disclosure matter.

**Strength: Moderate.** The connection itself is not disqualifying, but the omission — especially in the context of a partial disclosure of the same organization — is a cognizable disclosure deficiency. **Favors a challenge by the Claimant.**

### E. Undisclosed pending arbitration (Halcyon Biotech) and incomplete disclosure of prior appointments

**Facts.** The Arbitrator's Section III disclosure lists seven matters (six completed and one pending — *Orion Fabrication v. Mountain West Supply*, in which Barrow & Thistlewood LLP represents the respondent) and certifies that the list "represents a complete list of arbitrations in which I have served alongside counsel from either Barrow & Thistlewood LLP or Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP." The compiled appointment data shows that the Arbitrator is currently serving as sole arbitrator in **Halcyon Biotech v. Stonewall Partners**, a pending matter in which Barrow & Thistlewood LLP represents a party. That matter does not appear in the Disclosure Statement.

In addition, a matter-by-matter comparison of the Disclosure Statement against the appointment data's case list shows that the Arbitrator has completed **nine** arbitrations involving Barrow & Thistlewood LLP and **five** involving Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP (thirteen total, with one matter — *Westbrook Partners v. Arcadian Ventures* — involving both firms). The disclosure listed only **six** of those thirteen completed matters. The seven completed matters omitted from the disclosure include:

1. *Crestfield Energy v. Loran Industrial* (2022) — Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (claimant); award for claimant;
2. *Ashford Medical Devices v. Greenway Therapeutics* (2022) — Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (respondent); award for respondent;
3. *Westbrook Partners v. Arcadian Ventures* (2022) — Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP (claimant) and Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (respondent); award for respondent;
4. *Trident Coatings v. Bayshore Chemical* (2023) — Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (claimant); award for respondent;
5. *Redstone Logistics v. Fairfield Transport* (2023) — Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (respondent); award for respondent;
6. *Thornwall Systems v. Kessler Automation* (2023) — Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP (respondent); award for claimant; and
7. *Warwick Technologies v. Cypress Digital* (2023) — Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (claimant); award for claimant.

(The appointment data's summary row references "five" undisclosed matters; the matter-by-matter comparison set out above identifies seven completed matters omitted, in addition to the pending *Halcyon Biotech* matter.)

**Disclosure status.** Not disclosed. The Arbitrator's express representation that her list is "complete" is contradicted by the appointment data.

**Analysis.** Rule R-17(a)(iii) requires disclosure of "any prior or pending cases" involving the parties' counsel. The omission of a **pending** matter is particularly significant: a pending matter creates an ongoing relationship with counsel and a prospective income stream, which is relevant to the appearance of independence. The omission of seven completed matters — including one (*Westbrook*) in which **both** firms appeared before the Arbitrator as sole arbitrator — undermines the reliability of the disclosure and of the Arbitrator's certification of completeness. The pattern of omission is not neutral as to outcome: of the omitted Barrow & Thistlewood matters, the firm's clients prevailed in five of six (Crestfield, Ashford, Westbrook, Redstone, Warwick), with the firm's client losing only in Trident. Whether the omissions are inadvertent or selective, the result is that the disclosure understates both the volume and the favorable-outcome concentration of the Arbitrator's history with Respondent's counsel.

**Strength: Strong as a disclosure deficiency** (the certification of completeness is demonstrably inaccurate). **Favors a challenge by the Claimant** (the omissions concern the Respondent's counsel and, in the main, favorable outcomes for that counsel).

### F. Repeat-appointment pattern and economic dependence on Barrow & Thistlewood LLP

**Facts.** The compiled appointment data for the Arbitrator's last twenty completed arbitrations (2021–2024) shows:

- Barrow & Thistlewood LLP appeared in **9 of 20** completed matters (**45%**);
- Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP appeared in **5 of 20** completed matters (**25%**);
- The Barrow & Thistlewood client prevailed in **7 of 9** completed matters (**77.8%**);
- The Fielding Marsh client prevailed in **1 of 4** adjudicated matters (**25.0%**), with one matter settled;
- Estimated aggregate fees from Barrow & Thistlewood matters: **~\$1,020,000**, representing **~48.0%** of the Arbitrator's estimated total arbitration income of ~\$2,125,000 over the period;
- Estimated aggregate fees from Fielding Marsh matters: **~\$410,000** (~19.3%);
- Of the Arbitrator's five currently pending matters, **three** involve Barrow & Thistlewood LLP, representing an estimated **\$560,000** of **\$835,000** in pending fees (**67.1%**).

**Disclosure status.** Partially disclosed. The Arbitrator disclosed seven specific matters but did not disclose the aggregate concentration of her practice in Barrow & Thistlewood matters, the fee dependence, or the prevailing-rate asymmetry.

**Analysis.** Repeat appointments are common and, standing alone, generally not disqualifying. But the concentration here is substantial: a single firm accounts for nearly half of the Arbitrator's recent arbitration income and two-thirds of her pending fee exposure, and that firm's clients have prevailed in roughly three of every four completed matters before her. Under the broad "justifiable doubt" standard of Rule R-16 and the catch-all of Rule R-17(a)(iv), a reasonable party could question whether an arbitrator whose livelihood depends materially on continued engagement by one firm's practice can be entirely independent of that firm in a matter in which it appears. The economic-dependence theory of *Morelite* and the "appearance of bias" concern of *Commonwealth Coatings* are directly implicated. The Arbitrator's own disclosure — by understating the number of Barrow & Thistlewood matters and omitting the favorable-outcome concentration — prevented the parties from evaluating this pattern.

**Strength: Moderate to strong**, particularly in combination with the omitted pending *Halcyon* matter and the Lakeview referral relationship. **Favors a challenge by the Claimant.**

### G. Incomplete disclosure of the Lakeview Dispute Resolution Center affiliation

**Facts.** The Arbitrator discloses her affiliation with Lakeview Dispute Resolution Center as a panel neutral, identifies the Managing Director (Harold Lund), and states that Lakeview "receives case referrals from various law firms ... including from both Barrow & Thistlewood LLP and Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP." She does not describe the **financial structure** of the affiliation — whether Lakeview takes a percentage of the Arbitrator's fees, whether Lakeview's revenue depends on maintaining referral relationships with the firms, or whether the Arbitrator's income is in any way contingent on Lakeview's relationships with counsel. Lakeview also hosted the October 14, 2023 CLE at which the Arbitrator and Mr. Hargrave co-presented (see Part IV.C).

**Disclosure status.** Disclosed, but incompletely. The Arbitrator's conclusory statement that she "do[es] not believe that Lakeview's receipt of referrals from counsel for either party creates a conflict" does not substitute for disclosure of the financial terms that would allow the parties to form their own view.

**Analysis.** Rule R-17(a)(i) and (a)(ii) reach financial and business relationships. If Lakeview's business model depends on referrals from Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (the firm that accounts for ~48% of the Arbitrator's income and that referred the present matter's counsel relationship), and if the Arbitrator's compensation flows through or depends on Lakeview, there is an indirect economic interdependency that the parties should have been told about. The disclosure's partial treatment of this affiliation is a deficiency, though less severe than the outright omissions in Parts IV.A–E.

**Strength: Moderate.** **Favors a challenge by either party**, more acutely by the Claimant given Barrow & Thistlewood's dominant role as a referrer.

---

## V. Summary: Disclosed vs. Undisclosed

| # | Connection | Nature | Disclosed? | Primary rule | Favors challenge by |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | Indirect interest in Whitmore Fund II LP (via Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund, 2.3% LP) | Financial — indirect | **No** ("no direct equity positions" only) | R-17(a)(i) | Claimant |
| B | Spouse (Dr. Alan Fairhaven) co-authored with Claimant's expert Dr. Rita Peshkov (Sept 2022) | Professional/family — academic collaboration with a witness | **No** (and affirmative statement appears inaccurate) | R-17(a)(ii) | **Respondent** |
| C | Co-panelist with lead Respondent's counsel Hargrave, CLE on directly germane topic (Oct 2023) | Professional — co-panelist with counsel | **No** | R-17(a)(ii), (a)(iv) | Claimant |
| D | Shared organizational service with Respondent's principal Whitmore (MN ADR Ethics Institute Trustees/Advisory) | Professional — shared board service | **No** (Institute affiliation partly disclosed) | R-17(a)(ii) | Claimant |
| E | Pending *Halcyon Biotech* matter + 7 omitted completed matters involving the firms' counsel | Professional — undisclosed prior/pending appointments | **No** (certification of completeness inaccurate) | R-17(a)(iii) | Claimant |
| F | Repeat-appointment/fee concentration (B&T = 45% of matters, ~48% of income, 77.8% win rate) | Economic — dependence pattern | **Partial** (understated) | R-16, R-17(a)(iv) | Claimant |
| G | Lakeview affiliation — financial terms of referral relationship | Financial/institutional | **Partial** (terms not disclosed) | R-17(a)(i), (a)(ii) | Either (Claimant more so) |

Of the seven items, **five were not disclosed at all** (A, B, C, D, and the *Halcyon* component of E), **one was disclosed but incompletely as to its scope** (the prior-appointment component of E, and F), and **one was disclosed but incompletely as to its financial terms** (G). The Arbitrator's certification that her statement "represents all circumstances known to me that might give rise to justifiable doubt" is, on this record, not supportable.

---

## VI. Disclosure Adequacy and Vacatur Risk

**Adequacy of the disclosure.** The Disclosure Statement does not satisfy the breadth required by Rules R-16 and R-17. At least three of the omissions fall within categories the Rules expressly enumerate: an indirect financial interest in an entity affected by the result (R-17(a)(i)); a family member's academic collaboration with a witness (R-17(a)(ii)); and undisclosed prior/pending cases involving the parties' counsel (R-17(a)(iii)). The co-panelist relationship and the shared Institute service are additional R-17(a)(ii) relationships, and the CLE topic implicates R-17(a)(iv). The Arbitrator's continuing duty under R-17(b) is not satisfied by a disclosure that omits these items, and her certification of completeness is contradicted by the public record.

**Vacatur risk under FAA § 10(a)(2).** If the arbitration proceeds to an award with these deficiencies unaddressed, the resulting award faces a meaningful risk of vacatur on evident-partiality grounds. The strongest vacatur candidates are (i) the indirect financial interest in Whitmore Fund II LP (an interest in an entity affected by the result, undisclosed despite R-17(a)(i)'s express coverage of indirect interests), and (ii) the spouse–witness co-authorship (an undisclosed family/professional relationship with a central witness on the very technology in dispute). The repeat-appointment and fee-concentration data supply an economic-dependence theory akin to *Morelite*, and the pattern of non-disclosure echoes the "appearance of bias" concern of *Commonwealth Coatings*. A court reviewing an award need not find actual bias; evident partiality can rest on undisclosed relationships that a reasonable party would have wanted to know. The risk is not symmetrical: the connections to the Respondent (A, C, D, F) would support a Claimant vacatur motion, while the Peshkov co-authorship (B) would support a Respondent vacatur motion. Either way, proceeding with this Arbitrator without curing the disclosure creates avoidable award-enforcement risk for both sides.

**Waiver.** Under Rule R-18(f), a party does not waive grounds that "were not and could not reasonably have been known" within the challenge deadline. The items in Part IV were not disclosed and were discoverable only through independent public-records research; they therefore should not be deemed waived even if raised after June 24, 2024. The Rule also expressly preserves the right to assert evident partiality in a vacatur proceeding. Nonetheless, raising any challenge within the seven-day window is the safer course and preserves the full range of Rule R-18 remedies (including AAA-ordered replacement under R-19).

---

## VII. Strategic Assessment

**For the Respondent (our client).** The strongest ground available to Whitmore is **Part IV.B — the Peshkov co-authorship**. It is a clear, documentable disclosure failure under an expressly enumerated category; it implicates the Claimant's single most important expert witness; the subject matter of the co-authored paper is the technical core of the dispute; and the Arbitrator's affirmative statement that her husband has no professional relationship with Cascade personnel appears to be contradicted by the public record. This ground is independent of the connections between the Arbitrator and the Respondent and would not require our client to disavow its first-choice ranking on grounds that benefit it. If our client has any concern that the Arbitrator may be unduly inclined to credit Dr. Peshkov's testimony, this is the ground to press.

**Exposure to a Claimant challenge.** Our client should anticipate that Claimant's counsel may independently develop the public-record research and identify the connections between the Arbitrator and the Respondent — the indirect Fund II interest (A), the Whitmore trusteeship (D), the Hargrave CLE (C), the omitted *Halcyon* matter and prior appointments (E), and the fee-concentration pattern (F). These give the Claimant multiple, colorable grounds to challenge and, if an award issues, to seek vacatur. Because our client ranked the Arbitrator first, a successful Claimant challenge would remove the arbitrator our client preferred. Our client should therefore evaluate now whether the Peshkov concern (which cuts against us) is outweighed by the connections that cut in our favor, or whether a clean arbitrator without these entanglements on either side better serves the client's long-term interest in an enforceable award.

**The supplemental-disclosure option.** Before filing a challenge, Rule R-17(d) contemplates that "the AAA may, in its discretion, bring any disclosure deficiency to the attention of the arbitrator and request supplemental disclosure before acting on any challenge." A request (through the AAA) for a supplemental disclosure directed at each Part IV item would either (i) produce disclosures that allow both parties to make an informed waiver/objection decision, or (ii) confirm the Arbitrator's inability or unwillingness to cure, which would strengthen any challenge. This is a low-cost, low-risk first step and is consistent with the Arbitrator's own continuing duty under R-17(b).

---

## VIII. Recommended Next Steps

1. **By June 23, 2024** — Submit to the AAA a written request that the Arbitrator provide a supplemental disclosure under Rule R-17(b)/(d) addressing, specifically: (a) whether the Arbitrator holds any interest, direct or indirect, in the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund or any other vehicle with exposure to Whitmore Fund II LP or Volaris; (b) her spouse's co-authorship with Dr. Rita Peshkov and any other professional relationship between her spouse and Cascade personnel; (c) the October 14, 2023 CLE co-panelist relationship with Dominic Hargrave; (d) Garrett T. Whitmore's service on the Board of Trustees of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute; (e) the pending *Halcyon Biotech v. Stonewall Partners* matter and a complete list of prior arbitrations involving the parties' counsel; and (f) the financial terms of the Lakeview affiliation.

2. **By June 24, 2024** — Preserve the Respondent's right to challenge by filing, or holding in readiness, a written challenge under Rule R-18(a) stating with specificity the Peshkov co-authorship ground (Part IV.B), with the other items reserved. The decision whether to file the challenge versus await the supplemental disclosure should be made after consultation with the client, weighing the Peshkov concern against the connections that favor our client.

3. **Concurrently** — Prepare a response memorandum anticipating a Claimant challenge on the Part IV.A, C, D, E, and F grounds, and assess whether to oppose, take no position, or join aspects of a Claimant challenge.

4. **Throughout** — Document the public-record sources supporting each factual assertion (IRS Form 990; SEC/fund filings; academic database; CLE archives; AAA records) so that any challenge or later vacatur motion is evidentiarily supported.

5. **Reserve** — Expressly reserve all rights under Rule R-18(f) and FAA § 10(a)(2), including the right to seek vacatur on evident-partiality grounds for any undisclosed matter that was not and could not reasonably have been known within the challenge window.

---

# MEMORANDUM

**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL --- ATTORNEY-CLIENT PRIVILEGE / ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT**

  ----------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **TO:**                             Dominic Hargrave, Lead Partner --- Barrow & Thistlewood LLP

  **FROM:**                           Catherine M. Aldridge, Associate --- Barrow & Thistlewood LLP

  **DATE:**                           June 20, 2024

  **RE:**                             Assessment of Grounds for Challenge to the Arbitrator's Disclosure Statement --- AAA Case No. 01-24-0003217, *Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. v. Whitmore Capital Partners LLC*
  ----------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## I. Executive Summary

This memorandum assesses whether the June 17, 2024 Arbitrator Disclosure Statement of Hon. Margaret R. Fairhaven (Ret.) ("the Arbitrator") provides a basis for a challenge under AAA Commercial Arbitration Rules R-16 through R-18, the AAA/ABA Code of Ethics for Arbitrators in Commercial Disputes, the IBA Guidelines on Conflicts of Interest in International Arbitration, and Section 10(a)(2) of the Federal Arbitration Act. It is based on a comparison of the Disclosure Statement against the Arbitration Demand, the AAA rules excerpts, the compiled arbitrator appointment data, and the entity and relationship profiles prepared from public records.

**Bottom line.** The Disclosure Statement is materially deficient. Cross-referencing the Arbitrator's disclosures against the public-record research identifies **five categories of relationship or interest that were not disclosed**, an **incomplete account of the Arbitrator's prior and pending appointments** involving the parties' counsel, and an **incomplete description of the Arbitrator's institutional affiliation** with Lakeview Dispute Resolution Center. Several of these omissions fall squarely within categories that AAA Rule R-17 expressly requires be disclosed, and the Arbitrator's certification that her statement "represents all circumstances known to me" is, on the present record, inaccurate.

The undisclosed matters are not uniform in their strategic direction:

-   **One undisclosed connection favors a challenge by the Respondent (Whitmore):** the co-authorship of a peer-reviewed article by the Arbitrator's spouse and the Claimant's anticipated key expert witness, Dr. Rita Peshkov, on a topic that is the technical core of this arbitration. This is the cleanest single ground available to our client.
-   **Four undisclosed connections, together with the repeat-appointment pattern, favor a challenge by the Claimant (Cascade):** the Arbitrator's possible indirect financial interest in Whitmore Fund II LP (the fund that owns the licensee Volaris); the shared organizational service between the Arbitrator and Respondent's principal Garrett T. Whitmore at the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute; the co-panelist relationship between the Arbitrator and lead Respondent's counsel Dominic Hargrave at a CLE on a directly germane topic; and the omission of a pending arbitration in which Barrow & Thistlewood LLP appears.
-   **The repeat-appointment and fee-concentration data** (Barrow & Thistlewood LLP appeared in 45% of the Arbitrator's last 20 completed arbitrations and generated an estimated 48% of her arbitration income, with the firm's clients prevailing in 7 of 9 completed matters) bear on both sides' evaluation and on the risk of vacatur.

**Deadline.** Under Rule R-18(a), any challenge must be filed in writing with the AAA and served on opposing counsel within **seven (7) calendar days of the disclosure --- i.e., by June 24, 2024**. Because the deficiencies identified below were not disclosed and could not reasonably have been known to the parties from the Disclosure Statement itself, Rule R-18(f) preserves the right to raise them notwithstanding the deadline, and the right to assert evident partiality in any later vacatur proceeding under FAA § 10 is unaffected. Nonetheless, we recommend acting within the June 24 window to preserve all procedural options.

**Recommendation.** We recommend (1) promptly requesting a supplemental disclosure from the Arbitrator under Rule R-17(d) directed at each of the items in Part IV; (2) preserving our client's right to challenge on the Peshkov co-authorship ground by the June 24 deadline if the supplemental disclosure does not resolve the concern; and (3) preparing for the strong likelihood that Claimant's counsel will independently identify and challenge on the connections between the Arbitrator and the Respondent. The vacatur exposure under FAA § 10(a)(2) is material and should be weighed before any party elects to proceed with this Arbitrator.

## II. Procedural Framework and Governing Standards

### A. The AAA disclosure and challenge rules

**Rule R-16** establishes the governing standard: an arbitrator "shall disclose to the AAA any circumstance likely to give rise to justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator's impartiality or independence, including any bias or any financial or personal interest in the result of the arbitration, or any past or present relationship with the parties, their representatives, or their counsel." The Rule directs that the obligation "shall be construed broadly to further the goal of ensuring that arbitrators are and remain impartial and independent," and that the standard of "justifiable doubt" is "intended to encompass all circumstances that a reasonable person would consider relevant."

**Rule R-17(a)** enumerates four non-exclusive categories of required disclosure:

-   **(a)(i)** --- "Any known direct or indirect financial or personal interest in the outcome of the arbitration, including any interest, whether legal or beneficial, in the subject matter of the dispute, in any party, or in any entity that may be affected by the result of the proceeding";
-   **(a)(ii)** --- "Any known existing or past financial, business, professional, family, or social relationships with any party to the arbitration, its counsel, representatives, or witnesses that are likely to affect impartiality or that might reasonably create an appearance of partiality or bias," expressly "including ... relationships arising from employment, professional association, joint ventures, board memberships, academic collaboration, or regular social contact";
-   **(a)(iii)** --- "Any prior or pending cases in which the arbitrator has been or is involved ... with any party to the arbitration, its counsel, or representatives," extending to all matters regardless of institution or capacity; and
-   **(a)(iv)** --- a catch-all covering "any other circumstances that might cause a reasonable party to question the arbitrator's ability to render a fair and impartial determination," expressly "including but not limited to the arbitrator's prior publications, public statements, or expressed views on legal or factual issues relevant to the dispute."

**Rule R-17(b)** makes disclosure a **continuing obligation**; the arbitrator "shall not await inquiry by a party or by the AAA before making any required supplemental disclosure." **Rule R-17(d)** provides that the "failure of an arbitrator to make complete and timely disclosures ... may result in the arbitrator's removal in accordance with Rule R-18, and may constitute grounds for vacatur of any resulting award under applicable law, including but not limited to Section 10 of the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. § 10)."

**Rule R-18(a)** requires that any challenge be made "by written notice to the AAA and to the other party, stating with specificity the grounds for the challenge, within seven (7) calendar days after (i) the appointment of the challenged arbitrator has been communicated to the challenging party, or (ii) the circumstances giving rise to the challenge became known or should reasonably have become known to the challenging party, whichever is later." **Rule R-18(f)** provides that a party that fails to timely challenge "shall be deemed to have waived its right to challenge the arbitrator on the grounds then known to the party, except that a party shall not be deemed to have waived its right to challenge based on grounds that were not and could not reasonably have been known at the time of the deadline," and expressly preserves the right to seek vacatur on grounds of evident partiality "provided that such issues were not previously known and subject to waiver."

### B. The FAA and the "evident partiality" standard

Section 10(a)(2) of the Federal Arbitration Act, 9 U.S.C. § 10(a)(2), permits vacatur of an award "where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators." The Supreme Court in *Commonwealth Coatings Corp. v. Continental Casualty Co.*, 393 U.S. 145 (1968), held that arbitrators must "avoid the appearance of partiality" and disclose "any dealings that might create an impression of possible bias," recognizing that professional arbitrators with ongoing business relationships must nevertheless disclose connections that a reasonable party would want to know. The Second Circuit's decision in *Morelite Construction Corp. v. New York City District Council Carpenters Pension Funds*, 952 F.2d 16 (2d Cir. 1991), found evident partiality where an arbitrator's employer had a financial relationship with a party --- illustrating that **economic dependence** on a party (or, by extension, on a firm that regularly appears before the arbitrator) can supply evident partiality even without proof of actual bias.

### C. The Code of Ethics and the IBA Guidelines

The AAA rules excerpts direct attention to the **AAA/ABA Code of Ethics for Arbitrators in Commercial Disputes** (Canon I --- integrity and fairness; Canon II --- disclosure; Canon IV --- fair conduct) and the **IBA Guidelines on Conflicts of Interest in International Arbitration**. The IBA Guidelines' General Standards require disclosure of any fact that may, from a reasonable third party's perspective, give rise to doubts as to impartiality or independence, and categorize conflicts into a Non-Waivable Red List, a Waivable Red List, an Orange List (disclose; waivable if no objection), and a Green List (no disclosure needed). As discussed below, several of the undisclosed items here fall at least within Orange List territory (shared board/committee service, prior appointments, prior professional interaction with counsel), and the indirect financial interest approaches Red List treatment.

## III. Factual Background

The arbitration arises from a derivative and direct action by the minority shareholders of Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. ("Cascade") against Whitmore Capital Partners LLC ("Whitmore"), the manager of Whitmore Fund III LP, which holds a 62% controlling stake in Cascade. Claimants allege that Whitmore directed Cascade to license its proprietary "ThermalCore" heat-exchange technology to Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC ("Volaris") --- a portfolio company of a *different* Whitmore-managed fund, Whitmore Fund II LP --- at \$2.8 million per year, against an independently determined fair market value of \$9.5 million per year, yielding claimed damages of \$46.9 million over the seven-year license term. The claims sound in breach of fiduciary duty, self-dealing in violation of the Cascade Operating Agreement, unjust enrichment, and waste.

The Claimant is represented by **Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP** (lead counsel Priya Narayanan). The Respondent is represented by **Barrow & Thistlewood LLP** (lead partner Dominic Hargrave; Ellen Wu heads the firm's national arbitration practice and is involved in arbitrator-selection strategy for the matter). The Respondent's principal, **Garrett T. Whitmore**, personally directed the challenged transaction and is an anticipated fact witness. The Claimant's anticipated key expert witness is **Dr. Rita Peshkov**, Cascade's Vice President of R&D and the named inventor on most of the ThermalCore patents. Volaris (and its CEO Thomas Kinnear) is a non-party beneficiary of the disputed license and an anticipated non-party witness.

The Arbitrator was selected on June 3, 2024. During the ranking process, **Whitmore ranked the Arbitrator first** and Cascade ranked her third. The AAA confirmed the appointment on June 10, 2024, and the Arbitrator submitted her Disclosure Statement on June 17, 2024.

## IV. Assessment of Specific Grounds for Challenge

The following assessment addresses each potential ground, identifying the factual basis, the disclosure status, the applicable rule, the strength of the challenge, and the party for whom the ground is most salient.

### A. Undisclosed indirect financial interest in Whitmore Fund II LP (via the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund)

**Facts.** The Arbitrator discloses that her investment portfolio is managed on a discretionary basis by Northbridge Wealth Advisors and states that she "hold\[s\] no direct equity positions in any party to this arbitration." She further states that she "ha\[s\] not undertaken a position-by-position review of each fund or sub-investment held within \[her\] managed accounts." Public SEC filings and fund disclosure documents show that Northbridge Wealth Advisors also manages the **Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund**, a fund-of-funds vehicle whose most recent public filing (March 2024) reports a **2.3% limited partner interest in Whitmore Fund II LP**. Whitmore Fund II LP owns Volaris, the direct beneficiary of the disputed below-market license. The economic outcome of this arbitration --- any award rescinding, renegotiating, or repricing the Volaris license, or imposing damages on Whitmore --- would affect the value of Whitmore Fund II LP and, derivatively, the value of a limited partner interest in that fund.

**Disclosure status.** Not disclosed. The Arbitrator's "no direct equity positions" formulation is literally accurate but does not address **indirect** interests, which Rule R-17(a)(i) expressly reaches ("any ... direct or indirect financial ... interest ... in any party, or in any entity that may be affected by the result"). The Arbitrator identifies her manager (Northbridge) but does not identify the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund, its holdings, or the potential chain of indirect exposure to a fund managed by the Respondent.

**Analysis.** Two features are significant. First, the duty under R-17(a)(i) extends to indirect interests and to "any entity that may be affected by the result"; Whitmore Fund II LP is precisely such an entity, because it owns the licensee whose license is the subject of the dispute. Second, the Arbitrator's reliance on discretionary management and her statement that she has not reviewed her holdings do not discharge the disclosure obligation --- particularly where the very firm managing her portfolio offers a product invested in a fund managed by the Respondent, a fact that a diligent inquiry (which the Arbitrator certifies she undertook) should have surfaced. Whether the Arbitrator is in fact allocated to the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund is not confirmed by the public record; the point for challenge purposes is that the potential indirect interest was neither investigated nor disclosed, and the "no direct equity positions" language is susceptible to being read as misleading by omission. Under the IBA Guidelines, an interest in an entity affected by the result is at least an Orange List (and arguably Waivable Red List) matter requiring disclosure.

**Strength: Moderate to strong** (contingent on confirming the Arbitrator's allocation to the Select Alternatives Fund; the disclosure deficiency itself is clear). **Favors a challenge by the Claimant**, because the interest is economically aligned with the Respondent/Fund II side of the transaction.

### B. Undisclosed co-authorship between the Arbitrator's spouse and the Claimant's key expert witness (Dr. Rita Peshkov)

**Facts.** The Arbitrator discloses that her husband, Dr. Alan Fairhaven, is a retired professor of mechanical engineering at the University of Minnesota (faculty 1992--2019, emeritus since), and states that "to the best of my knowledge, he has no financial or professional relationship with Cascade Thermal Systems Inc., Whitmore Capital Partners LLC, or any of their officers, directors, or employees." Academic publication databases show that Dr. Alan Fairhaven **co-authored a peer-reviewed article with Dr. Rita Peshkov** --- Cascade's Vice President of R&D and the Claimant's anticipated key expert witness --- titled "Optimization of Phase-Change Heat Exchangers in High-Load Industrial Applications," published in the *Journal of Advanced Thermal Engineering*, Vol. 42, Issue 3 (September 2022). The subject matter --- phase-change heat exchangers in high-load industrial applications --- is the technical core of the ThermalCore technology at issue in this arbitration.

**Disclosure status.** Not disclosed. Moreover, the Arbitrator's affirmative statement that her husband "has no ... professional relationship with Cascade ... or any of their officers, directors, or employees" appears to be **inaccurate**: Dr. Peshkov is an officer and employee of Cascade, and the co-authorship reflects a professional (academic collaboration) relationship. Co-authorship of a peer-reviewed article ordinarily reflects a sustained collaboration --- joint research design, data collection, drafting, and revision --- and the September 2022 publication indicates a relationship that predated the arbitration and may have continued.

**Analysis.** Rule R-17(a)(ii) requires disclosure of "professional, family, or social relationships with any party ... its counsel, representatives, or **witnesses**," expressly including "academic collaboration." The relationship here runs from the Arbitrator's **spouse** to a **witness**, but the disclosure obligation is construed broadly and plainly reaches family members' professional relationships with witnesses --- particularly where the witness is central and the subject matter overlaps. The Arbitrator will be called upon to assess Dr. Peshkov's credibility and the weight of her expert testimony on the very technology that Dr. Peshkov and the Arbitrator's husband jointly researched and published on. The appearance that the Arbitrator may be predisposed to credit (or, at minimum, is not institutionally distant from) that testimony is exactly the kind of "appearance of partiality" Rule R-17(a)(ii) targets. The IBA Guidelines treat a family member's professional relationship with a witness as a disclosable Orange List matter; the subject-matter nexus and the witness's centrality elevate its seriousness.

**Strength: Strong.** This is the single cleanest ground for a challenge by the Respondent: it is a clear, documentable disclosure failure under an expressly enumerated category (R-17(a)(ii)), it implicates the Claimant's key expert, and the Arbitrator's affirmative statement about her husband's lack of professional relationships with Cascade personnel appears to be contradicted by the public record. **Favors a challenge by the Respondent.**

### C. Undisclosed co-panelist relationship with lead Respondent's counsel (Dominic Hargrave) on a directly germane CLE topic

**Facts.** On October 14, 2023 --- approximately five months before the arbitration demand and eight months before the Arbitrator's appointment --- the Lakeview Dispute Resolution Center hosted a CLE panel titled "Arbitrating Complex IP Licensing Disputes." The listed panelists included the Arbitrator and **Dominic Hargrave**, the lead partner for the Respondent in this arbitration (alongside Professor Naomi Strickland and Marcus DelVecchio). The topic is directly germane to this arbitration, which concerns the valuation and enforceability of a technology license. The event was held at Lakeview, the Arbitrator's own affiliated center.

**Disclosure status.** Not disclosed. The Arbitrator's disclosure addresses prior *arbitrations* in which Barrow & Thistlewood LLP appeared, but does not disclose this co-panelist relationship with Mr. Hargrave specifically.

**Analysis.** Rule R-17(a)(ii) reaches professional relationships with counsel; Rule R-17(a)(iv) reaches "public statements, or expressed views on legal or factual issues relevant to the dispute." Co-panelist service involves shared preparation and interaction, and the panel's topic --- arbitrating complex IP licensing disputes --- is the subject of this case. Standing alone, a single CLE co-panelist event is a relatively modest professional contact (IBA Orange List). It gains force here from three features: (i) the topic is directly germane; (ii) the event was hosted at the Arbitrator's own institution (Lakeview), suggesting a closer nexus than a neutral venue; and (iii) it was not disclosed. The non-disclosure is the actionable element: a reasonable party would want to know that its arbitrator and opposing lead counsel recently co-presented on the very subject of the dispute.

**Strength: Moderate.** Most powerful in combination with the other connections to the Respondent's counsel (the repeat-appointment pattern, the Lakeview referral relationship, and the shared Institute service). **Favors a challenge by the Claimant.**

### D. Undisclosed shared organizational service with the Respondent's principal (Garrett T. Whitmore) at the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute

**Facts.** The Arbitrator discloses that she has served on the **advisory board** of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute since 2021, and that **Ellen Wu** of Barrow & Thistlewood LLP also serves on that advisory board. The Arbitrator does **not** disclose that **Garrett T. Whitmore** --- the Respondent's founder, Managing Partner, and the individual who personally directed the challenged transaction and is an anticipated witness --- serves on the **Board of Trustees** of the same Institute, as shown by the Institute's IRS Form 990 for fiscal year 2023. The Board of Trustees is the Institute's governing body, responsible for strategic direction, budgetary oversight, and appointments to leadership positions, including the advisory board on which the Arbitrator sits. The Institute is described as a small nonprofit; its bylaws and posted minutes indicate that the Board of Trustees and the advisory board hold annual meetings and periodic joint sessions.

**Disclosure status.** Not disclosed. Notably, the Arbitrator disclosed the Institute affiliation and Ms. Wu's advisory board service, but omitted the Respondent's principal's role on the governing body of the same organization.

**Analysis.** Rule R-17(a)(ii) requires disclosure of professional relationships with a party (or party representative) arising from "board memberships." The Arbitrator and the Respondent's principal serve the same small nonprofit in overlapping governance roles, with the Trustees body formally superior to (and appointing) the advisory board on which the Arbitrator serves. The connection is somewhat attenuated --- shared nonprofit board service is common and, disclosed, would often be waivable --- but the **failure to disclose** is the problem, particularly given that the Arbitrator opened the topic by disclosing the Institute affiliation and Ms. Wu's role yet omitted the party principal's trusteeship. A reasonable party would consider the Respondent's principal's governing role at the Arbitrator's organization relevant to evaluating the Arbitrator's independence. Under the IBA Guidelines, shared committee/board service in a small organization is an Orange List disclosure matter.

**Strength: Moderate.** The connection itself is not disqualifying, but the omission --- especially in the context of a partial disclosure of the same organization --- is a cognizable disclosure deficiency. **Favors a challenge by the Claimant.**

### E. Undisclosed pending arbitration (Halcyon Biotech) and incomplete disclosure of prior appointments

**Facts.** The Arbitrator's Section III disclosure lists seven matters (six completed and one pending --- *Orion Fabrication v. Mountain West Supply*, in which Barrow & Thistlewood LLP represents the respondent) and certifies that the list "represents a complete list of arbitrations in which I have served alongside counsel from either Barrow & Thistlewood LLP or Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP." The compiled appointment data shows that the Arbitrator is currently serving as sole arbitrator in **Halcyon Biotech v. Stonewall Partners**, a pending matter in which Barrow & Thistlewood LLP represents a party. That matter does not appear in the Disclosure Statement.

In addition, a matter-by-matter comparison of the Disclosure Statement against the appointment data's case list shows that the Arbitrator has completed **nine** arbitrations involving Barrow & Thistlewood LLP and **five** involving Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP (thirteen total, with one matter --- *Westbrook Partners v. Arcadian Ventures* --- involving both firms). The disclosure listed only **six** of those thirteen completed matters. The seven completed matters omitted from the disclosure include:

1.  *Crestfield Energy v. Loran Industrial* (2022) --- Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (claimant); award for claimant;
2.  *Ashford Medical Devices v. Greenway Therapeutics* (2022) --- Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (respondent); award for respondent;
3.  *Westbrook Partners v. Arcadian Ventures* (2022) --- Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP (claimant) and Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (respondent); award for respondent;
4.  *Trident Coatings v. Bayshore Chemical* (2023) --- Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (claimant); award for respondent;
5.  *Redstone Logistics v. Fairfield Transport* (2023) --- Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (respondent); award for respondent;
6.  *Thornwall Systems v. Kessler Automation* (2023) --- Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP (respondent); award for claimant; and
7.  *Warwick Technologies v. Cypress Digital* (2023) --- Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (claimant); award for claimant.

(The appointment data's summary row references "five" undisclosed matters; the matter-by-matter comparison set out above identifies seven completed matters omitted, in addition to the pending *Halcyon Biotech* matter.)

**Disclosure status.** Not disclosed. The Arbitrator's express representation that her list is "complete" is contradicted by the appointment data.

**Analysis.** Rule R-17(a)(iii) requires disclosure of "any prior or pending cases" involving the parties' counsel. The omission of a **pending** matter is particularly significant: a pending matter creates an ongoing relationship with counsel and a prospective income stream, which is relevant to the appearance of independence. The omission of seven completed matters --- including one (*Westbrook*) in which **both** firms appeared before the Arbitrator as sole arbitrator --- undermines the reliability of the disclosure and of the Arbitrator's certification of completeness. The pattern of omission is not neutral as to outcome: of the omitted Barrow & Thistlewood matters, the firm's clients prevailed in five of six (Crestfield, Ashford, Westbrook, Redstone, Warwick), with the firm's client losing only in Trident. Whether the omissions are inadvertent or selective, the result is that the disclosure understates both the volume and the favorable-outcome concentration of the Arbitrator's history with Respondent's counsel.

**Strength: Strong as a disclosure deficiency** (the certification of completeness is demonstrably inaccurate). **Favors a challenge by the Claimant** (the omissions concern the Respondent's counsel and, in the main, favorable outcomes for that counsel).

### F. Repeat-appointment pattern and economic dependence on Barrow & Thistlewood LLP

**Facts.** The compiled appointment data for the Arbitrator's last twenty completed arbitrations (2021--2024) shows:

-   Barrow & Thistlewood LLP appeared in **9 of 20** completed matters (**45%**);
-   Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP appeared in **5 of 20** completed matters (**25%**);
-   The Barrow & Thistlewood client prevailed in **7 of 9** completed matters (**77.8%**);
-   The Fielding Marsh client prevailed in **1 of 4** adjudicated matters (**25.0%**), with one matter settled;
-   Estimated aggregate fees from Barrow & Thistlewood matters: **~\$1,020,000,\ representing~ 48.0%** of the Arbitrator's estimated total arbitration income of \~\$2,125,000 over the period;
-   Estimated aggregate fees from Fielding Marsh matters: \*\*~\$410,000\*\*\ (~19.3%);
-   Of the Arbitrator's five currently pending matters, **three** involve Barrow & Thistlewood LLP, representing an estimated **\$560,000** of **\$835,000** in pending fees (**67.1%**).

**Disclosure status.** Partially disclosed. The Arbitrator disclosed seven specific matters but did not disclose the aggregate concentration of her practice in Barrow & Thistlewood matters, the fee dependence, or the prevailing-rate asymmetry.

**Analysis.** Repeat appointments are common and, standing alone, generally not disqualifying. But the concentration here is substantial: a single firm accounts for nearly half of the Arbitrator's recent arbitration income and two-thirds of her pending fee exposure, and that firm's clients have prevailed in roughly three of every four completed matters before her. Under the broad "justifiable doubt" standard of Rule R-16 and the catch-all of Rule R-17(a)(iv), a reasonable party could question whether an arbitrator whose livelihood depends materially on continued engagement by one firm's practice can be entirely independent of that firm in a matter in which it appears. The economic-dependence theory of *Morelite* and the "appearance of bias" concern of *Commonwealth Coatings* are directly implicated. The Arbitrator's own disclosure --- by understating the number of Barrow & Thistlewood matters and omitting the favorable-outcome concentration --- prevented the parties from evaluating this pattern.

**Strength: Moderate to strong**, particularly in combination with the omitted pending *Halcyon* matter and the Lakeview referral relationship. **Favors a challenge by the Claimant.**

### G. Incomplete disclosure of the Lakeview Dispute Resolution Center affiliation

**Facts.** The Arbitrator discloses her affiliation with Lakeview Dispute Resolution Center as a panel neutral, identifies the Managing Director (Harold Lund), and states that Lakeview "receives case referrals from various law firms ... including from both Barrow & Thistlewood LLP and Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP." She does not describe the **financial structure** of the affiliation --- whether Lakeview takes a percentage of the Arbitrator's fees, whether Lakeview's revenue depends on maintaining referral relationships with the firms, or whether the Arbitrator's income is in any way contingent on Lakeview's relationships with counsel. Lakeview also hosted the October 14, 2023 CLE at which the Arbitrator and Mr. Hargrave co-presented (see Part IV.C).

**Disclosure status.** Disclosed, but incompletely. The Arbitrator's conclusory statement that she "do\[es\] not believe that Lakeview's receipt of referrals from counsel for either party creates a conflict" does not substitute for disclosure of the financial terms that would allow the parties to form their own view.

**Analysis.** Rule R-17(a)(i) and (a)(ii) reach financial and business relationships. If Lakeview's business model depends on referrals from Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (the firm that accounts for \~48% of the Arbitrator's income and that referred the present matter's counsel relationship), and if the Arbitrator's compensation flows through or depends on Lakeview, there is an indirect economic interdependency that the parties should have been told about. The disclosure's partial treatment of this affiliation is a deficiency, though less severe than the outright omissions in Parts IV.A--E.

**Strength: Moderate.** **Favors a challenge by either party**, more acutely by the Claimant given Barrow & Thistlewood's dominant role as a referrer.

## V. Summary: Disclosed vs. Undisclosed

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  ----------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------- ---------------------- ---------------------------
  A           Indirect interest in Whitmore Fund II LP (via Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund, 2.3% LP)                     Financial --- indirect                                          **No** ("no direct equity positions" only)              R-17(a)(i)             Claimant

  B           Spouse (Dr. Alan Fairhaven) co-authored with Claimant's expert Dr. Rita Peshkov (Sept 2022)                      Professional/family --- academic collaboration with a witness   **No** (and affirmative statement appears inaccurate)   R-17(a)(ii)            **Respondent**

  C           Co-panelist with lead Respondent's counsel Hargrave, CLE on directly germane topic (Oct 2023)                    Professional --- co-panelist with counsel                       **No**                                                  R-17(a)(ii), (a)(iv)   Claimant

  D           Shared organizational service with Respondent's principal Whitmore (MN ADR Ethics Institute Trustees/Advisory)   Professional --- shared board service                           **No** (Institute affiliation partly disclosed)         R-17(a)(ii)            Claimant

  E           Pending *Halcyon Biotech* matter + 7 omitted completed matters involving the firms' counsel                      Professional --- undisclosed prior/pending appointments         **No** (certification of completeness inaccurate)       R-17(a)(iii)           Claimant
